us-iran-ceasefire-mou-signed-june-17-hor.headline: US-Iran ceasefire MOU signed June 17; Hormuz re-closed June 20 after Israeli Lebanon strikes; US Navy announced 'widened route' June 27 — strait status remains contested
us-iran-ceasefire-mou-signed-june-17-hor.summary: The June 17 US–Iran 60-day ceasefire MOU has not restored normal Strait of Hormuz traffic; analysts warn flows may not normalize until 2027 even under the best outcome, with elevated war-risk insurance premiums, lingering sea mines, and Iran's structural leverage unchanged. Brent has fallen from a ~$188 wartime peak to ~$72, but the physical market gap and inventory rebuild cycle keep oil price risk two-sided.
us-iran-ceasefire-mou-signed-june-17-hor.target_indicator: Hormuz/Red Sea status (0 open / 1 contested / 2 closed)
us-iran-ceasefire-mou-signed-june-17-hor.rationale: The MOU has not reopened Hormuz to normal commercial traffic. WTO data cited by the UK Parliament shows a 95% reduction in crude carriers and 99% in LNG since the conflict began. The UAE's state oil company does not expect full flows until 2027. The Hormuz/Red Sea status indicator should move from 1 (contested) toward 2, and the WTI sub-indicator should reflect the ~30% post-peak collapse from >$100 to ~$69, with residual re-escalation risk from Lebanon ceasefire breakdown or nuclear sticking points. All three s20 indicators (Hormuz status, WTI, cable incidents) are materially updated.

boj-raises-policy-rate-to-1-0-at-june-16.headline: BOJ raises policy rate to 1.0% at June 16 MPM; hawkish board members call for hikes every 'few months' toward 2% neutral; 10-year JGB yield at ~2.61–2.65%
boj-raises-policy-rate-to-1-0-at-june-16.summary: The Bank of Japan lifted its policy rate to 1.0% on June 16 and simultaneously cut its JGB purchase plan to ~¥200bn/month. BOJ Deputy Governor Himino and board member Tamura both signaled further hikes every few months targeting a ~2% neutral rate, with Tamura warning that if upside inflation risks intensify the BOJ should 'accelerate the pace of rate hikes without hesitation.'
boj-raises-policy-rate-to-1-0-at-june-16.rationale: The June 16 BOJ rate hike to 1% — combined with accelerated QT (JGB purchases halved to ¥200bn/month) and explicit board rhetoric toward a 2% neutral — is a step-change for the carry trade and JGB market. The 10-year JGB is at 2.61–2.65%, a multi-decade high that, combined with Japan's fiscal arithmetic, pressures carry-trade unwinds and USD/JPY volatility. The 30-year JGB yield indicator and Takaichi plan financing clarity indicators both need updating. The July 31 MPM is the next critical watch date.

china-blacklists-mp-materials-and-usa-ra.headline: China blacklists MP Materials and USA Rare Earth on June 22 — targeting the two US government-backed domestic producers with categorical dual-use export bans
china-blacklists-mp-materials-and-usa-ra.summary: On June 22, China's MOFCOM placed 10 US entities on its export control list, with MP Materials (Mountain Pass mine) and USA Rare Earth (Texas mine-to-magnet) being the strategically significant targets; the action bars any entity worldwide from transferring Chinese-origin dual-use goods to these firms — a tighter 'categorical prohibition' vs. the prior license-based regime. Simultaneously, China's Finance Ministry barred ~46 US firms from Chinese government procurement.

proposed_indicator.label: US domestic RE producer blacklist status (0 none / 1 entity-specific ban / 2 sector-wide ban)
proposed_indicator.rubric: 0 = no US rare-earth producers on China's export control entity list; 1 = named entity-specific bans in place (current: MP Materials + USA Rare Earth blacklisted, barring Chinese dual-use equipment/tech transfers globally); 2 = China escalates to sector-wide prohibition covering all US-linked rare-earth processors or imposes production quota cuts targeting Western supply chains. Sources: MOFCOM entity list updates, company SEC filings, USGS supply-chain reports.

china-blacklists-mp-materials-and-usa-ra.rationale: The existing s6 indicators track export license flow and the Nov-2025 pause expiry, but do not capture the new 'categorical entity designation' instrument that China is now deploying. Blacklisting the US government's two flagship domestic rare-earth producers directly attacks the strategic diversification effort — cutting off their access to Chinese processing equipment, IP, and tech transfers — and signals willingness to escalate to sector-wide controls. This new instrument warrants a dedicated indicator, especially with the November 10 pause window approaching and neodymium-praseodymium oxide prices up ~6x since January 2026.

genius-act-aml-sanctions-rulemaking-adva.headline: GENIUS Act AML/sanctions rulemaking advanced by Treasury/FinCEN (April 9) ahead of July 18 regulatory deadline; stablecoin supply and T-bill demand implications crystallizing
genius-act-aml-sanctions-rulemaking-adva.summary: The GENIUS Act (signed July 18, 2025) mandated all federal implementing regulations by July 18, 2026 — one week away. FinCEN and OFAC jointly proposed the AML/sanctions compliance rule in April 2026. Brookings notes stablecoin issuers' Treasury bill and reverse-repo demand is growing structurally, while four open regulatory issues (interest/yield prohibition, interoperability, bank vs. nonbank competition, and fraud-victim recovery) remain unresolved and could create friction at the go-live deadline.
genius-act-aml-sanctions-rulemaking-adva.target_indicator: GENIUS implementation friction (0 smooth / 1 gaps / 2 problems)
genius-act-aml-sanctions-rulemaking-adva.rationale: The July 18 regulatory deadline is the single most important near-term implementation milestone for the GENIUS Act. With four significant open issues unresolved per Brookings and the AML rule still only proposed (not final), the 'GENIUS implementation friction' indicator should be moved from 0 (smooth) to 1 (gaps), as final rules may not be promulgated on time, creating legal uncertainty for issuers and impacting the stablecoin supply trajectory and its Treasury demand implications.

bofa-estimates-ai-hyperscaler-capex-appr.headline: BofA estimates AI hyperscaler CapEx approaching $800bn in 2026 (+67% YoY), trending toward $1 trillion in 2027; capex-vs-revenue gap concern intensifying
bofa-estimates-ai-hyperscaler-capex-appr.summary: BofA Global Research estimates hyperscaler capital expenditures could approach $800 billion in 2026, a 67% increase from 2025, trending toward $1 trillion in 2027; investor scrutiny over whether economic returns will justify this level of investment and how quickly spending translates into sustained earnings growth remains the key risk, with Amundi flagging AI capex as the main equity support even as macro uncertainty rises.
bofa-estimates-ai-hyperscaler-capex-appr.target_indicator: AI capex-vs-revenue gap concern (0 closing / 1 wide / 2 widening fast)
bofa-estimates-ai-hyperscaler-capex-appr.rationale: An $800bn capex run-rate (+67% YoY) with investor uncertainty explicitly flagged by major sell-side and buy-side houses moves the capex-vs-revenue gap indicator firmly to '2 — widening fast.' The scale now implies massive debt issuance by hyperscalers (relevant to US IG OAS in s8) and grid/power demand acceleration (relevant to s15_ai_power). At ~$1 trillion in 2027 projections, this is no longer a sector risk but a macro balance-sheet risk — especially if the Middle East shock raises cost of capital while revenue ramp lags.

eba-june-2026-risk-assessment-eu-eea-hea.headline: EBA June 2026 Risk Assessment: EU/EEA headline inflation rising to 3.0–3.1% on energy shock; EA GDP slowing to 0.9%; bank-NBFI linkages up 10.4% YoY — energy-driven credit risk spreading beyond directly exposed sectors
eba-june-2026-risk-assessment-eu-eea-hea.summary: The EBA's June 2026 Risk Assessment Report finds euro area inflation rising from 2.1% to 3.0% (EU: 2.5% to 3.1%) driven by Middle East energy prices, EA GDP growth slowing to just 0.9%, and EU bank exposures to non-bank financial institutions (NBFIs) rising 10.4% YoY — creating a compounding channel where energy inflation, tighter monetary conditions, and bank-NBFI linkages could trigger broader asset-quality deterioration beyond directly energy-exposed portfolios.

proposed_story.name: European energy-stagflation & bank-NBFI contagion channel
proposed_story.why: The Middle East conflict has created a persistent energy-price shock that is simultaneously driving euro area inflation back above 3%, compressing EA GDP to near-stagnation (0.9%), forcing the ECB into a hawkish pause, and widening EU bank exposures to NBFIs by over 10% YoY. This creates a novel compounding risk loop not captured by the existing s10_france story (which focuses on OAT-Bund spreads and political stability) nor s20_sea_lanes (which tracks Hormuz status and WTI). The EBA explicitly flags that second-order credit-quality deterioration — spreading beyond directly energy-exposed sectors via tighter financial conditions and bank-NBFI linkages — is the key systemic threat to EU banking in 2H 2026.

indicators.label: Euro area HICP energy component YoY (%)
indicators.rubric: Tracks the energy sub-index of euro area HICP inflation (Eurostat monthly release). Values >10% signal acute pass-through risk to core inflation and ECB hawkish pivot; 5–10% = elevated; <5% = contained. Current: materially elevated driven by post-Hormuz oil/gas prices.

indicators.label: EU bank-to-NBFI exposure growth YoY (%)
indicators.rubric: Tracks YoY growth in EU/EEA bank exposures to non-bank financial institutions (OFC category in EBA FINREP data). >10% = rising linkage risk (current: +10.4%); >20% = systemic concern; <5% = stable. A rapid build-up amplifies contagion in a stress scenario where NBFI assets (private credit, leveraged loans) come under pressure.

indicators.label: EA GDP growth vs ECB rate path divergence (0 aligned / 1 diverging / 2 stagflation trap)
indicators.rubric: 0 = ECB easing in line with slowing growth (soft landing); 1 = ECB on hold or hiking while EA GDP <1.5% (current: EA at 0.9%, ECB hawkish pause); 2 = EA GDP contracting while ECB holds/hikes due to persistent energy inflation — stagflation scenario with acute credit deterioration risk across household and corporate portfolios.

eba-june-2026-risk-assessment-eu-eea-hea.rationale: The EBA's June 2026 RAR is a primary supervisory source, not a market opinion piece. It documents a materializing feedback loop: Middle East energy shock → euro area inflation reacceleration → ECB hawkish pause → compressed growth → widening bank-NBFI linkages → latent credit deterioration risk across EU bank portfolios. This is structurally distinct from s10_france (political fragility) and s20_sea_lanes (Hormuz status/WTI): it is a second-order financial-stability channel specific to EU/EEA banking. With EA GDP at 0.9% and Euribor rates moving upward simultaneously, the risk of a EU-specific stagflation credit event in 2H 2026 is material and currently untracked.

us-iran-ceasefire-collapses-for-4th-cons.headline: US–Iran ceasefire collapses for 4th consecutive day; Iran attacks Bahrain & Kuwait, Hormuz status contested
us-iran-ceasefire-collapses-for-4th-cons.summary: The US–Iran Islamabad Memorandum of Understanding signed June 17 established a 60-day negotiating window, but Iran re-closed the strait within days after Israel continued Lebanon strikes. As of early July, WTO data shows a ~95% reduction in crude-oil tanker transits and ~99% reduction in LNG since the February 28 conflict began; the US naval blockade remains, oil remains above $100/bbl, and the UAE's national oil company says full Hormuz flows won't recover until 2027 even with a quick deal.
us-iran-ceasefire-collapses-for-4th-cons.target_indicator: Hormuz/Red Sea status (0 open / 1 contested / 2 closed)
us-iran-ceasefire-collapses-for-4th-cons.rationale: The Hormuz/Red Sea status indicator in s20 should be set to 2 (effectively closed for practical purposes — 95% crude reduction, 99% LNG reduction per WTO data), with WTI well above tracker norms and the UAE ADNOC CEO explicitly forecasting no full recovery until 2027. The MOU was immediately violated, negotiations are stuck on nuclear terms and sovereignty, and the US blockade remains in force. This is the single highest-materiality live event in the tracker, feeding directly into s18 (fertilizer), s4 (EM debt via energy-price squeeze), and s2 (Japan energy import costs and yen weakness).

boj-officials-signal-rate-hikes-every-fe.headline: BOJ officials signal rate hikes every few months; 30yr JGB yields at 3.88%, 10yr at 2.61% — carry trade unwind risk acute
boj-officials-signal-rate-hikes-every-fe.summary: Japan's benchmark 10y JGB yield reached 2.88% on July 9 — the highest since September 1996 — driven by oil-price inflation from the Iran war, fiscal expansion fears under the Takaichi plan (¥370 trillion roadmap), and a brief political 'Basic Policy shock' when the government appeared set to discourage future BoJ rate hikes; the 30-year yield touched 4.005%. Finance Minister Katayama's July 10 remarks urging GPIF to rotate into domestic assets partially steadied markets.
boj-officials-signal-rate-hikes-every-fe.rationale: The 30y JGB yield (tracker indicator) briefly hit 4.005% — its highest in decades — with the 10y touching a 30-year high of 2.88%. Both primary indicators for s2 are flashing: the Takaichi plan financing clarity indicator should move toward 2 (disorderly) given the 'Basic Policy shock' episode where government language nearly signalled interference with BoJ rate decisions, before being walked back July 10. The GPIF-rotation comment adds a new carry-trade unwind dimension. The tracker should update both the 30y JGB value and Takaichi financing clarity (1→2 then partially back to 1).

china-blacklists-mp-materials-and-usa-ra.headline: China blacklists MP Materials and USA Rare Earth — first entity-specific ban on US domestic RE producers effective June 22
china-blacklists-mp-materials-and-usa-ra.summary: In June 2026 China added 10 US entities to its export control list including the two largest US domestic rare-earth producers, MP Materials and USA Rare Earth, prohibiting any party globally from transferring Chinese-origin dual-use RE items to them. Separately, MOFCOM Announcement No. 26 (effective July 1) formalises a public reporting mechanism for export-control violations, signalling active enforcement escalation.
china-blacklists-mp-materials-and-usa-ra.target_indicator: US domestic RE producer blacklist (0 none / 1 entity-specific ban / 2 sector-wide ban)
china-blacklists-mp-materials-and-usa-ra.rationale: The blacklisting of MP Materials and USA Rare Earth — the two cornerstone US domestic RE producers that DoD has backed with $400M+ in contracts — directly triggers the 'US domestic RE producer blacklist' indicator, moving it from 0 to 1 (entity-specific ban). This is particularly material because it targets the exact companies the US is relying on to build supply-chain independence. Combined with the July 1 MOFCOM enforcement mechanism, the Nov-2025 pause (status indicator) is being effectively circumvented via entity-level actions ahead of the Nov-2026 expiry.

hyperscaler-ai-capex-approaches-800bn-in.headline: Hyperscaler AI capex approaches $800bn in 2026 (+67% YoY), trending toward $1 trillion in 2027 — revenue gap concern widens
hyperscaler-ai-capex-approaches-800bn-in.summary: BofA Global Research (June 15 CIO letter) estimates hyperscaler capex could reach $800 billion in 2026, a 67% increase from 2025, and trend toward $1 trillion in 2027. Investor scrutiny remains high over whether economic returns will justify the spending, with broad AI infrastructure debt and concentration risk elevated.
hyperscaler-ai-capex-approaches-800bn-in.target_indicator: AI capex-vs-revenue gap concern (0 closing / 1 wide / 2 widening fast)
hyperscaler-ai-capex-approaches-800bn-in.rationale: BofA's estimate of $800bn in hyperscaler capex for 2026 (+67% YoY) directly updates the s8_ai_capex story. The gap concern indicator should be marked '2 widening fast': capex is scaling geometrically while revenue is not matching pace. The BofA CIO letter itself flags investor scrutiny over 'whether the economic return will ultimately justify this level of investment.' The BlackRock macro outlook similarly notes that 'the AI-enthralled market narrative' echoes prior euphoria cycles. The scale of debt issuance underpinning these data-center builds is the systemic link to the 'GPU-backed debt refinancing stress' and US IG OAS proxies in the same story.

iran-claims-sovereign-control-over-hormu.headline: Iran claims sovereign control over Hormuz maritime management post-MOU — structural chokepoint sovereignty risk now embedded in ceasefire terms
iran-claims-sovereign-control-over-hormu.summary: The June 17 US–Iran MOU allows Hormuz transit 'with no charge for 60 days only,' after which Iran and Oman will determine 'future administration and maritime services.' Iran's IRGC also redefined Hormuz as a 'vast operational area' extending from Jask to Siri Island in May 2026. Multiple analysts warn Iran has achieved de-facto leverage over global oil flow regardless of the final deal.

proposed_indicator.label: Hormuz sovereignty/toll risk (0 free passage / 1 contested management / 2 Iran toll/control enforced)
proposed_indicator.rubric: 0 = Hormuz operates under international freedom-of-navigation norms with no state claiming administrative control; 1 = MOU or ceasefire terms grant Iran/Oman co-management rights, transit operationally possible but under negotiated terms with 60-day sunset clauses and recurring re-escalation risk; 2 = Iran actively enforcing tolls, restricting allied nation shipping, or formally asserting sovereign control recognized in international frameworks. Current reading: 1. Review trigger: expiry of 60-day MOU window (~mid-August 2026) or new IRGC interdiction incident.

iran-claims-sovereign-control-over-hormu.rationale: The existing s20_sea_lanes story tracks whether Hormuz is 'open/contested/closed' on an operational basis, but the June 17 MOU introduces a structurally new risk dimension: Iran has extracted a clause giving it co-management and future toll rights over the world's most critical oil chokepoint. This is not captured by the binary open/closed indicator. Even when Hormuz is operationally transitable, embedded toll-and-sovereignty provisions create a recurring extortion risk for oil importers (Japan, India, South Korea, China) and a potential WTO/UNCLOS challenge. A separate indicator for this structural shift is warranted to distinguish between 'ships are moving' (operational) and 'Iran has legitimized control' (strategic/structural).

genius-act-implementation-rulemaking-und.headline: GENIUS Act implementation rulemaking underway — OCC/Treasury/FinCEN NPRMs active, 18-month compliance clock running since July 2025
genius-act-implementation-rulemaking-und.summary: The GENIUS Act (signed July 18, 2025) is now in multi-agency implementation: OCC issued proposed rules on stablecoin issuance requirements, while Treasury/FinCEN/OFAC issued joint AML/sanctions NPRMs. The effective date is the earlier of January 2027 or 120 days after final rules are issued, but rulemaking complexity and interagency coordination gaps could cause friction ahead of the deadline.
genius-act-implementation-rulemaking-und.target_indicator: GENIUS implementation friction (0 smooth / 1 gaps / 2 problems)
genius-act-implementation-rulemaking-und.rationale: The GENIUS Act is law (July 2025) but the OCC, FinCEN, and OFAC are still in proposed-rulemaking phase as of mid-2026, with the compliance clock running toward a January 2027 hard deadline. The OCC bulletin confirms that the majority of rules are proposed but not finalized; FinCEN/OFAC AML/sanctions rules were issued separately in April 2026, still in comment period. This multi-agency divergence in rulemaking timelines — and the challenge of coordinating federal vs. state supervisory regimes under §10 billion thresholds — places this indicator at '1 gaps' rather than '0 smooth'. The risk is that issuers face conflicting or delayed final guidance, delaying the stablecoin Treasury-demand dynamic the tracker monitors. Current total USD stablecoin supply is growing rapidly (28%+ YoY per WEF data), making regulatory clarity increasingly urgent.

japan-10y-jgb-yield-at-2-65-yen-at-40-ye.headline: Japan 10y JGB yield at ~2.65%, yen at 40-year low ~161/USD as BOJ slows QT taper
japan-10y-jgb-yield-at-2-65-yen-at-40-ye.summary: Japan's 10-year JGB yield has risen to ~2.65–2.67% (near 30-year highs) as the yen has sunk to ~161 per dollar, its weakest in four decades, even as the BOJ at its June 17 meeting slowed the pace of bond tapering from ¥400bn to ¥200bn per quarter at maturities up to 10 years. BOJ Governor Ueda and board member Tamura both reiterated support for further rate hikes, with the next decision on July 31, keeping carry-trade unwind risk elevated.
japan-10y-jgb-yield-at-2-65-yen-at-40-ye.target_indicator: USD/JPY and 30-year JGB yield — update: 10y at 2.65%, USD/JPY ~161 (40-year yen low); BOJ slowed QT taper Jun 17, next decision Jul 31. Takaichi plan financing clarity: 1 unclear (¥370T 2040 public-private investment target reported by Nikkei, adding bond-issuance fear).
japan-10y-jgb-yield-at-2-65-yen-at-40-ye.rationale: All three existing s2 indicators are moving adversely in the same week: the yen is at a 40-year low (heightening carry-unwind risk), 10y JGB yields are near multi-decade highs (raising debt-service cost on Japan's ~250% debt/GDP), and the BOJ's June 17 taper slowdown signals it is still constrained in normalizing — a classic fiscal dominance signal. The Nikkei-reported ¥370T investment target adds financing uncertainty. Materiality is high because Japanese repatriation flows can tighten global sovereign yields, especially US Treasuries.

china-blacklists-mp-materials-and-usa-ra.headline: China blacklists MP Materials and USA Rare Earth on June 22, targeting US domestic producers
china-blacklists-mp-materials-and-usa-ra.summary: China's MOFCOM on June 22, 2026 added 10 US entities — including MP Materials (Mountain Pass mine) and USA Rare Earth — to its export control list, effectively barring Chinese firms from supplying dual-use items to them and prohibiting any third-party globally from transferring Chinese-origin dual-use goods to the named companies. China's Finance Ministry simultaneously barred ~46 US firms (Lockheed, Boeing, General Atomics subsidiaries) from government procurement, framed as retaliation for the Pentagon's June expansion of its Chinese-military-companies list.
china-blacklists-mp-materials-and-usa-ra.target_indicator: US domestic RE producer blacklist — update: escalate from 0 (none) to 1 (entity-specific ban). MP Materials and USA Rare Earth — the two flagship US government-backed domestic producers — are now on China's export control list with an effective categorical prohibition (not merely a licensing hurdle) on receiving Chinese-origin dual-use inputs. The broader Nov-2025 pause (status: 0 extended) holds through Nov 10, 2026, but this entity action sits outside that pause scope.
china-blacklists-mp-materials-and-usa-ra.rationale: This is a direct hit on the US domestic rare-earth build-out strategy: the two companies most central to reducing US-China dependency (MP Materials at Mountain Pass, USA Rare Earth in Texas) are now blocked from Chinese equipment and technology inputs. The existing tracker's 'US domestic RE producer blacklist' indicator was designed for exactly this scenario and should move from 0 to 1. It also signals Beijing's willingness to escalate toward sector-wide restrictions (moving toward a 2), and creates financing/partnership risk for both companies. Defense, EV, and semiconductor supply chains are directly exposed.

genius-act-implementing-regulations-miss.headline: GENIUS Act implementing regulations missed key June/July 2026 deadlines; Tether reciprocity still unresolved
genius-act-implementing-regulations-miss.summary: As of late May 2026, no primary federal regulator (OCC, Fed, FDIC) had finalized GENIUS Act rules, against a statutory deadline of July 18, 2026 (18 months after enactment), meaning the effective compliance date defaults to January 2027 at the earliest. Tether's reciprocity determination — required for USDT to continue serving US businesses — has also not been issued, creating a potential supply disruption for the world's largest stablecoin.
genius-act-implementing-regulations-miss.target_indicator: GENIUS implementation friction — update: move from 0 (smooth) to 1 (gaps). The primary rulemaking deadline of July 18, 2026 is being missed by all three federal regulators; Tether's foreign-issuer reciprocity determination remains outstanding as of May 2026; multiple parallel agency rulemakings are running in parallel without coordination finalization. The effective date slips to January 2027 unless rules are finalized within 120 days from whenever they are issued.
genius-act-implementing-regulations-miss.rationale: The GENIUS Act was designed partly to mandate Treasury-bill-backed stablecoin reserves, potentially creating a new structural bid for short-dated US government paper. Regulatory slippage delays that demand channel and extends legal ambiguity for ~$250B+ in stablecoin supply. Tether's unresolved status is particularly material: USDT is the dominant global stablecoin and its forced restructuring or US-market exit would be a significant liquidity event. The tracker's existing friction indicator is the right home, and should move to 1 (gaps).

us-fy2026-deficit-hits-1-2-trillion-in-8.headline: US FY2026 deficit hits $1.2 trillion in 8 months, on track for $2T+, as OBBBA adds $3.4T over 10 years
us-fy2026-deficit-hits-1-2-trillion-in-8.summary: The US Treasury confirmed a $1.2 trillion federal deficit for the first eight months of FY2026 (October 2025–May 2026), including $293 billion in May alone, with Treasury's own projections flagging a $2 trillion-plus full-year outcome. The One Big Beautiful Bill Act (signed July 4, 2025) scored by CBO at $3.4 trillion in additional deficits over 10 years — with spending cuts deferred to 2027 — has pushed 10-year Treasury yields to ~4.48% and the Yale Budget Lab projects debt/GDP reaching 183% by 2054 under OBBBA.

proposed_indicator.label: US_fiscal_trajectory_score
proposed_indicator.rubric: 0 = deficit on declining path, debt/GDP stabilizing; 1 = deficit stable but elevated (>$1.5T/yr), debt/GDP drifting up; 2 = deficit accelerating (>$2T/yr), OBBBA-driven structural deterioration, term premium rising >50bps vs prior year. Current reading: 2.

us-fy2026-deficit-hits-1-2-trillion-in-8.rationale: The existing s4_dev_debt story tracks developing-world debt via the broad dollar index, US 10y real yield, and IMF program count. But the US fiscal trajectory itself is now a primary global macro risk driver: OBBBA-induced $3.4T in additional 10-year deficits, a $2T+ current-year gap, and CBO/Yale projections of debt/GDP at 156–183% by 2054 are materially pushing up US real yields (the s4 indicator) and threatening the dollar's reserve-currency premium. A dedicated US fiscal trajectory indicator captures the upstream driver that is causing the real-yield and dollar moves already in s4, and is not currently explicitly tracked. Materiality is high because rising US term premium spills directly into EM borrowing costs and global financial conditions.

us-iran-conflict-onset-feb-27-drives-fed.headline: US-Iran conflict (onset Feb 27) drives Fed toward rate hikes, EU inflation re-accelerates to 3%, global growth cut to 2.5%
us-iran-conflict-onset-feb-27-drives-fed.summary: A US-Iran military conflict that began on February 27, 2026 has triggered sharp energy price increases that are forcing a global macro regime shift: the Fed's June 2026 projections now show median expectations for one to two rate hikes in 2026 (vs. earlier cut expectations), eurozone headline inflation is forecast to rise from 2.1% to 3.0% in 2026, EU GDP growth has been cut to just 0.9%, and the World Bank has trimmed global growth to 2.5%. Oil prices have partially eased as US-Iran peace talks progress, but supply uncertainty remains.

proposed_story.name: US-Iran conflict & Middle East energy shock
proposed_story.why: A direct US-Iran military conflict begun February 27, 2026 has created a persistent energy-price shock that is simultaneously re-inflating advanced economies, forcing the Fed toward rate hikes rather than cuts, compressing EU growth to near-stagnation, raising Middle East geopolitical tail risk (including potential Hormuz disruption beyond the s20 lane-status indicator), and cutting global growth to 2.5%. This is a discrete new geopolitical event not captured by any single existing story — it interacts with s20 (sea lanes/Hormuz), s18 (food/fertilizer via energy input costs), s10 (France/eurozone fragility via energy-driven inflation), and s2 (Japan via energy import costs), but is the common upstream driver across all of them.

indicators.label: US_Iran_ceasefire_status
indicators.rubric: 0 = formal ceasefire / peace agreement in force; 1 = active hostilities paused / talks ongoing but fragile; 2 = active hostilities ongoing or re-escalation. Current reading: 1 (talks progressing, oil back near pre-war levels per Jun 29 data, but no formal agreement).

indicators.label: Middle_East_energy_shock_CPI_premium_bps
indicators.rubric: Basis points of CPI inflation attributable to Middle East energy shock vs. pre-conflict baseline (EBA/ECB estimates). Threshold: >50bps = elevated; >100bps = severe. Current: EU headline up ~90bps vs. prior forecast (2.1% to 3.0%).

indicators.label: Fed_rate_hike_probability_2026
indicators.rubric: Market-implied probability of at least one Fed rate hike by Dec 2026 (%). Threshold: <20% = benign; 20-50% = elevated; >50% = acute tightening risk. Current: Fed June SEP median shows 1-2 hikes, implying >50%.

us-iran-conflict-onset-feb-27-drives-fed.rationale: The US-Iran conflict is a discrete new geopolitical shock not present in the existing tracker at all. It is the single most important new macro development of 2026 H1: it has inverted the Fed's rate path from cuts to potential hikes, re-inflated the eurozone above target, compressed EU GDP growth to 0.9%, and knocked 0.3pp off the World Bank's global growth forecast. While s20 tracks sea-lane status and WTI, it does not capture the monetary-policy transmission of the shock (Fed hike risk, EU stagflation), which is the most systemically important channel. A new story is warranted to track the conflict's macro-financial ramifications distinct from the physical supply-chain angle.

hyperscaler-ai-capex-approaching-800b-in.headline: Hyperscaler AI capex approaching $800B in 2026 (+67% YoY), regulatory and revenue-gap scrutiny intensifying
hyperscaler-ai-capex-approaching-800b-in.summary: BofA Global Research estimates hyperscaler capital expenditures could approach $800 billion in 2026 — a 67% increase from 2025 — trending toward $1 trillion in 2027, while investor scrutiny over whether economic returns will justify this level of spending remains acute. The concentration of this capex in a small number of mega-cap issuers means any shift in sentiment or financing conditions carries outsized market risk.
hyperscaler-ai-capex-approaching-800b-in.target_indicator: AI capex-vs-revenue gap concern — update: move from 1 (wide) toward 2 (widening fast). BofA's $800B 2026 hyperscaler capex estimate (+67% YoY) with no commensurate revenue confirmation benchmark shifts the gap indicator. US IG OAS proxy: monitor for spread widening as large-scale debt issuance by hyperscalers (Microsoft, Google, Amazon, Meta) to fund capex continues to increase supply.
hyperscaler-ai-capex-approaching-800b-in.rationale: The $800B figure from a major sell-side house (BofA, dated June 15, 2026) is a new high-water mark for AI infrastructure spending estimates, a 67% single-year jump. This directly feeds s8's core concern: circular financing where debt-funded capex is justified by AI revenue projections that have not yet materialized. The existing capex-vs-revenue gap indicator should be updated, and the GPU-backed debt refinancing stress indicator should be flagged as a watch item given the scale of new issuance. Materiality is medium (not yet acute) but the trajectory is the concern.

china-blacklists-mp-materials-usa-rare-e.headline: China blacklists MP Materials & USA Rare Earth — entity-specific export control ban on key US domestic RE producers (June 22, 2026)
china-blacklists-mp-materials-usa-rare-e.summary: On June 22, China added 10 US entities to its export control list — including MP Materials (Mountain Pass, CA) and USA Rare Earth — barring Chinese companies from supplying dual-use items to them and prohibiting any global party from transferring Chinese-origin materials to these firms. Separately, effective July 1, China activated a formal public reporting mechanism (Announcement 26/2026) for violations of strategic-mineral export controls, including via third-country routing — a major escalation in enforcement architecture.
china-blacklists-mp-materials-usa-rare-e.rationale: The tracker's 'US domestic RE producer blacklist' indicator (rubric: 0 none / 1 entity-specific ban / 2 sector-wide ban) should move from 1 to at least 1+ approaching 2: China has now specifically targeted MP Materials and USA Rare Earth — the two largest US-based rare-earth operators — and the July 1 whistleblower enforcement mechanism extends reach to third-country routing, making circumvention far harder. This is qualitatively new in the last 7 days (the June 22 blacklisting and the July 1 enforcement regime are both within the window). The Nov-2025 pause status indicator remains at 0 (extended until Nov 2026) but the blacklist indicator has escalated materially.

strait-of-hormuz-partial-reopening-in-ea.headline: Strait of Hormuz: partial reopening in early June after ~3-month near-total blockade, but mines uncleared and flows well below pre-war levels (as of late June 2026)
strait-of-hormuz-partial-reopening-in-ea.summary: The US launched a fourth wave of strikes on Iran (July 12-13) after Iran attacked a Cyprus-flagged container ship transiting the Strait; Iran declared passage 'not possible,' crude tanker traffic effectively halted (zero large-vessel AIS transits on July 7-8), and Brent surged 4% to ~$79 on July 13 — shattering the fragile ceasefire MOU signed last month. Global oil output remains 9.4 million bpd below pre-war levels despite a partial June recovery.
strait-of-hormuz-partial-reopening-in-ea.rationale: The Hormuz/Red Sea status indicator is the primary watch-point for s20. Status has deteriorated from 'contested' (1) to effectively 'closed' (2) for the fourth time in seven days: Iran declared the strait shut, zero large-vessel AIS transits recorded July 7-8, and a fresh US strike wave July 12-13 shattered the ceasefire MOU. WTI rose to ~$74-76 and Brent to $78-79. This is a direct, real-time read on the indicator; the tracker should move Hormuz status to 2 (closed/contested) and flag acute oil-price risk premium.

fed-chair-warsh-abolishes-forward-guidan.headline: Fed Chair Warsh abolishes forward guidance at first FOMC meeting (June 17); hawkish dot plot; largest front-end UST selloff at an FOMC event since 2008
fed-chair-warsh-abolishes-forward-guidan.summary: At his inaugural FOMC meeting on June 17, new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh eliminated forward guidance entirely, slashed the policy statement to 132 words, and declined to submit his own dot-plot projection — while nine of eighteen other participants projected at least one rate hike in 2026. MUFG described the event as triggering 'the largest front-end UST selloffs since 2008' at an FOMC event, with 10-year Treasury yields jumping intraday to 4.49%. Warsh launched five task forces to overhaul Fed communications, balance sheet, data, AI/productivity, and inflation frameworks.

proposed_indicator.label: Fed communication regime (0 forward-guidance / 1 data-dependent ambiguity / 2 active opacity)
proposed_indicator.rubric: 0 = Fed provides explicit forward guidance and dot-plot with chair participation (pre-Warsh norm); 1 = forward guidance dropped, chair abstains from dot-plot, data-dependency declared — market reaction function unclear (current state); 2 = dot-plot formally abandoned, active suppression of communication, policy surprise rate elevated (>2 surprises per year)

fed-chair-warsh-abolishes-forward-guidan.rationale: The abolition of forward guidance is a structural regime shift in the primary mechanism through which the Fed suppresses short-rate volatility — a direct input into s1_basis_trade (MOVE index) and s21_clearinghouses (Treasury-clearing rollout stress). The MOVE index and front-end UST volatility are already tracked, but there is no indicator capturing the Fed's communication regime itself, which is now the independent variable driving those measures. The hawkish dot skew (9/18 see hikes) also materially re-prices the risk-free rate baseline for s3_private_credit, s8_ai_capex, and s9_cre maturity walls. A story_gap rather than new_risk because the transmission runs through existing stories, but the causal mechanism (communication opacity) is untracked.

china-fixed-asset-investment-collapsed-4.headline: China fixed-asset investment collapsed 4.1% YoY in Jan–May 2026, steepest drop since COVID; property investment down 16.2%
china-fixed-asset-investment-collapsed-4.summary: Official Chinese data released in June showed fixed-asset investment fell 4.1% year-on-year in the first five months of 2026 — the worst reading since the height of the COVID-19 pandemic in May 2020 — driven by a 16.2% plunge in property investment. Manufacturing investment rose only 0.4%, consistent with overcapacity concerns, while exports surged 19.4% in May (partly stockpiling ahead of further supply-chain disruption), deepening the deflation-via-export-surplus dynamic.

proposed_indicator.label: China fixed-asset investment YoY (%, Jan–May rolling)
proposed_indicator.rubric: Tracks the pace of domestic demand creation; readings above 0% = demand support for prices; 0 to -3% = mild contraction / deflationary risk; below -3% = acute domestic demand collapse reinforcing export-surplus deflation spillover. Current: -4.1% (acute zone). Sources: National Bureau of Statistics of China, monthly release.

china-fixed-asset-investment-collapsed-4.rationale: The existing s5_china_deflation story tracks PPI, USD/CNY, and tariff actions, but has no indicator for the underlying domestic demand weakness that is the root cause of the deflationary export surplus. A -4.1% FAI print — worst since COVID — combined with -16.2% property investment materially worsens the China PPI trajectory (more oversupply, more export dumping) and is a leading indicator for further CNY depreciation pressure and new anti-dumping actions globally. The story_gap is the absence of a domestic-demand health indicator within s5.

japan-yen-hits-4-decade-lows-10yr-jgb-at.headline: Japan yen hits 4-decade lows; 10yr JGB at ~2.65%; BoJ July 31 hike decision imminent amid Middle East energy import stress
japan-yen-hits-4-decade-lows-10yr-jgb-at.summary: The yen fell to its weakest level since the mid-1980s, with USD/JPY trading near 160-162 as rate differentials remain wide; BOJ board member Tamura called for hikes every few months toward a 2% neutral rate, while Finance Minister Katayama warned of 'appropriate' government response on July 3.
japan-yen-hits-4-decade-lows-10yr-jgb-at.rationale: USD/JPY near 162 is the weakest yen in four decades, squarely hitting the s2_japan USD/JPY indicator. BOJ Deputy Governor Tamura's explicit call for hikes 'every few months' toward 2% and the Finance Minister's July 3 intervention warning materially raise the probability of a sudden yen reversal and carry-trade unwind. The 10-year JGB has climbed above 2.7%, also moving the 30-year JGB yield indicator. Risk is clearly worsening (higher USD/JPY number = risk per rubric).

hormuz-disruption-to-fertilizer-inputs-3.headline: Hormuz disruption to fertilizer inputs: >30% of global urea and ~45% of global sulfur exports halted; WFP warns of food-crisis repeat akin to 2022
hormuz-disruption-to-fertilizer-inputs-3.summary: The 2026 Iran war effectively shut the Strait of Hormuz from late February, disrupting roughly 20% of world oil supply and triggering a urea/fertilizer shortage (Qatar halted LNG liquefaction; Iran agreed only in late March to allow humanitarian/fertilizer shipments). The IMF's July 2026 WEO Update flags a cluster of food-security and social-unrest risks in sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia directly linked to the energy-fertilizer nexus, while fertilizer input costs remain elevated ahead of Q3 planting seasons in the Southern Hemisphere.
hormuz-disruption-to-fertilizer-inputs-3.rationale: The 'Hormuz/Gulf disruption to fertilizer inputs' indicator (0 clear / 1 disrupted / 2 cut) should move to 2 (cut) — or remain at 2 and be flagged as re-escalating given the fresh July 7-13 fighting. Qatar's LNG liquefaction shutdown (fertilizer feedstock) and widespread urea shortages have already been confirmed. The IMF July WEO explicitly flags food-insecurity clustering in vulnerable economies as a near-term risk triggered by the Middle East conflict. The tracker should also note that the food-price trend indicator is approaching 2 (spiking) given the compounding supply shock.

strait-of-hormuz-remains-effectively-clo.headline: Strait of Hormuz Remains Effectively Closed — Brent ~$105/bbl, OECD Oil Inventories at 2003 Lows
strait-of-hormuz-remains-effectively-clo.summary: Since February 28, 2026, Iranian forces have blocked the Strait of Hormuz following U.S.-Israel strikes on Iran, cutting ~11 mb/d of Middle Eastern output and driving Brent to average ~$105/bbl in June–July. The EIA reports OECD oil inventories are the lowest since 2003, with a projected 7.6 mb/d inventory draw in Q3 2026.
strait-of-hormuz-remains-effectively-clo.target_indicator: Hormuz/Red Sea status (0 open / 1 contested / 2 closed)
strait-of-hormuz-remains-effectively-clo.rationale: The Strait of Hormuz is the dominant active risk driving global macro right now. The indicator 'Hormuz/Red Sea status' maps directly and should now read 2 (closed). WTI/Brent crude ($105/bbl) also updates the WTI crude indicator. This is the most material single development of the past 7 days and prior weeks, with OECD inventories at 20-year lows and a $3.7 mb/d deficit projected for Q2 2026.

hormuz-closure-severs-30-46-of-global-se.headline: Hormuz Closure Severs ~30–46% of Global Seaborne Fertilizer Supply; Food Inflation Risk Escalating
hormuz-closure-severs-30-46-of-global-se.summary: The OECD June 2026 Economic Outlook and World Bank flag the Iran conflict's energy shock as the primary global macro risk, with Gulf energy infrastructure damaged, Hormuz LNG flows at near zero, and headline inflation rising across advanced economies. Secondary effects on food and fertilizer (Persian Gulf fertilizer inputs disrupted) are accelerating; the Hormuz closure also disrupted LPG/ammonia feedstock flows that supply South and Southeast Asian fertilizer production.

proposed_indicator.label: Hormuz/Gulf disruption to fertilizer inputs
proposed_indicator.rubric: 0 = Strait open, fertilizer flows normal; 1 = partial disruption or re-routing, costs rising; 2 = Strait effectively closed (current), Gulf fertilizer exports blocked

hormuz-closure-severs-30-46-of-global-se.rationale: s18_food_fertilizer already has a 'Hormuz/Gulf disruption to fertilizer inputs' indicator but it was set to 0 (clear) at tracker inception. The Iran war and Hormuz closure have materially activated this indicator: Gulf LNG/LPG exports (critical for ammonia synthesis in Asia) collapsed alongside crude, and OECD explicitly flags food security impacts. This indicator needs to move to 2 (cut) given WTO data showing 95% drop in tanker traffic and confirmed LNG shutdowns. This is not captured by the existing 'Food-price trend' or 'China fertilizer export curbs' indicators.

japan-yen-at-4-decade-low-160-usd-despit.headline: Japan Yen at 4-Decade Low (~160/USD) Despite $73bn Intervention; BOJ 10y JGB Hits Near-30-Year High at 2.7%+
japan-yen-at-4-decade-low-160-usd-despit.summary: Japan spent over ¥11.7 trillion ($72.8bn) in FX intervention from April–May 2026 and raised rates to a multi-decade high, yet USD/JPY remains pinned near 160. The 10-year JGB yield climbed to ~2.7% (highest in ~30 years), and the 30-year JGB hit 3.88% in late June, as PM Takaichi's reflationary stance and dovish BOJ board nominees cloud the policy outlook.
japan-yen-at-4-decade-low-160-usd-despit.rationale: USD/JPY near 160 is a four-decade extreme despite massive intervention and a rate hike — a direct update to s2's USD/JPY indicator (risk = high, rate is elevated not lower). The 30y JGB at 3.88% and 10y at 2.7%+ simultaneously update the '30-year JGB yield' indicator. The BOJ policy conflict with PM Takaichi's reflationary nominations raises 'Takaichi plan financing clarity' to 2 (disorderly signals). All three s2 indicators need updating.

u-s-tariff-authority-cliff-section-122-e.headline: U.S. Tariff Authority Cliff: Section 122 Expires July 24 — Administration Pivoting to Sections 301 & 232
u-s-tariff-authority-cliff-section-122-e.summary: After the Supreme Court struck down IEEPA tariffs in February 2026 and the Court of International Trade ruled Section 122 tariffs illegal (May 7), the Section 122 10% across-the-board surcharge automatically expires July 24, 2026. The administration is now racing to reconstruct tariff barriers via expanded Sections 301 and 232 investigations covering 16 countries and ~75% of U.S. imports, with final determinations expected in July 2026.

proposed_story.name: US Tariff Legal Architecture Collapse & Reconstruction
proposed_story.why: The legal basis for ~60% of U.S. import tariff revenue has been twice struck down (IEEPA Feb 2026, Section 122 ruled illegal May 2026 and expiring July 24). The administration is simultaneously rebuilding tariff walls via Sections 301/232 covering virtually all U.S. imports, creating acute policy uncertainty for trade flows, supply chains, inflation, and EM export economies. A July 24 expiration without Congressional extension or new executive orders triggers an abrupt tariff collapse that markets have not priced.

indicators.label: Section 122 expiry / successor authority status (0 = stable legal basis / 1 = gap risk / 2 = authority lapsed or contested)
indicators.rubric: 0 = Congress extends or new authority firmly in place; 1 = active legal challenge, transition underway; 2 = authority lapses with no replacement, tariff revenue collapses

indicators.label: Average effective US tariff rate (%)
indicators.rubric: Tracks post-legal-challenge effective rate (was 27% peak Apr 2025, fell to ~11.8% by Apr 2026); large drop signals demand destruction reversal and inflation relief but revenue shock

indicators.label: Section 301/232 investigation pace (0 = normal / 1 = accelerated / 2 = emergency final rules)
indicators.rubric: 0 = investigations proceeding on normal timelines; 1 = expedited, covering >50% of imports; 2 = emergency final determinations covering >75% of imports issued ahead of July 24 expiry

u-s-tariff-authority-cliff-section-122-e.rationale: No existing tracker story captures the systemic legal fragility of U.S. tariff architecture. The July 24, 2026 Section 122 expiry is a hard binary event within the next 22 days. Legal reconstruction via Sections 301/232 covering nearly all U.S. imports is an escalation with no clear precedent. The risk is two-sided: expiry creates an inflationary deflation shock (tariff revenues collapse, import surges); aggressive Section 301/232 replacement risks WTO retaliation and further court challenges. This is structurally distinct from existing trade war stories and has a hard near-term trigger date.

genius-act-implementing-regulations-miss.headline: GENIUS Act Implementing Regulations Miss July 18 Deadline — Six Agencies Still Finalizing Rules
genius-act-implementing-regulations-miss.summary: The GENIUS Act (enacted July 18, 2025) mandated that six federal agencies finalize stablecoin rules within 18 months or 120 days after final regulations, whichever is earlier — a statutory deadline of July 18, 2026. As of early July 2026, no agency has issued final rules, with comment periods only closed as of June 9, placing the entire regulatory apparatus for a ~$250bn+ stablecoin market in legal limbo. Tether's foreign-issuer Treasury determination also remains unissued.
genius-act-implementing-regulations-miss.target_indicator: GENIUS implementation friction (0 smooth / 1 gaps / 2 problems)
genius-act-implementing-regulations-miss.rationale: The GENIUS Act's July 18, 2026 statutory implementation deadline is being missed — six agencies have not finalized rules as of the current date (July 2, 2026). This directly triggers the 'GENIUS implementation friction' indicator from 0 to at least 1 (gaps) and arguably 2 (problems), given that Tether's foreign-issuer determination is also unresolved. The effective date defaulting to January 2027 creates a 6-month limbo period for the $250bn+ stablecoin market, relevant to Treasury demand and the broader s7 story.

world-bank-cuts-em-global-growth-to-2-5-.headline: World Bank Cuts EM/Global Growth to 2.5% for 2026; Developing-World Borrowing Costs Surge on Energy Shock
world-bank-cuts-em-global-growth-to-2-5-.summary: The World Bank's June 2026 Global Economic Prospects cuts global growth to 2.5% and flags that rising EMDE borrowing costs — driven by Middle East energy prices at their highest since 2022 — are undermining fiscal space in the most indebted developing economies. Low-income country growth is revised down 0.3pp to 5.4%, with fiscal pressures threatening debt servicing capacity.
world-bank-cuts-em-global-growth-to-2-5-.target_indicator: New IMF programs / missed coupons (0 calm / 1 rising / 2 cluster)
world-bank-cuts-em-global-growth-to-2-5-.rationale: The World Bank's formal downgrade of global and EM growth, explicitly citing energy shock-driven tightening of EMDE financial conditions and rising debt servicing stress, is a material update to s4_dev_debt. The combination of higher oil-import bills, weaker EM currencies, and compressed fiscal space raises the probability of new IMF programs and missed coupons from the most vulnerable LICs, pushing the 'New IMF programs / missed coupons' indicator from 0 toward 1 (rising). The broad USD dollar index and 10y real yield indicators within s4 also need updating given the energy-shock inflation dynamics.

iran-re-asserts-route-control-over-strai.headline: Iran re-asserts route control over Strait of Hormuz days into fragile ceasefire; oil near $72 but shipping volumes at ~30% of pre-war levels
iran-re-asserts-route-control-over-strai.summary: On July 2, 2026, Iran's joint military command warned all tankers to use Tehran-approved routes or face 'a forceful response,' just hours after US-Iranian diplomats met in Qatar. Commercial transits remain far below pre-war levels of ~130 vessels/day, insurance remains prohibitively expensive, and analysts warn oil markets are underpricing persistent supply risk.
iran-re-asserts-route-control-over-strai.target_indicator: Hormuz/Red Sea status (0 open / 1 contested / 2 closed)
iran-re-asserts-route-control-over-strai.rationale: Hormuz remains the primary open story; the July 2 Iranian route-control ultimatum is a fresh escalation within the ongoing ceasefire window. The strait is currently 'contested' (score 1→borderline 2): Iran controls routing, transits are ~30% of normal, and a formal toll/control regime is being negotiated. WTI/Brent at ~$72 has fallen sharply from the ~$188 wartime high but analysts say inventory-rebuilding demand could push back toward $80. The Hormuz/Red Sea status indicator and WTI crude indicator both need updating; the undersea-cable sub-indicator is also elevated given mine-laying reports.

yen-craters-to-4-decade-low-30-year-jgb-.headline: Yen craters to 4-decade low; 30-year JGB yield rises to ~4.04%, highest since 1999 — carry-trade unwind risk acute
yen-craters-to-4-decade-low-30-year-jgb-.summary: As of July 1-2, 2026, USD/JPY hit levels not seen in four decades while the 30-year JGB yield touched 4.04% — up ~108bps over the past year. The BOJ faces the impossible trinity of defending the yen, capping JGB yields, and normalising policy amid Middle East-driven energy inflation, with a new dovish board appointee signalling conflicting policy signals.
yen-craters-to-4-decade-low-30-year-jgb-.rationale: The 30yr JGB at 4.04% and the yen at a 40-year low are simultaneous extremes that directly update both the USD/JPY and 30yr JGB yield indicators in s2_japan. The Takaichi plan financing clarity indicator should also move to '2 disorderly' given the new dovish BOJ board appointment (Ayano Sato) contradicting Ueda's hawkish tightening rhetoric, creating governance uncertainty. This is not a new story but the magnitude (4-decade yen low + 30yr JGB near 4%) materially worsens the carry-trade unwind risk tracked under s2.

china-s-november-2026-extraterritorial-r.headline: China's November 2026 extraterritorial rare-earth enforcement deadline looms; US rare-earth imports still 85%+ below 2024 levels for controlled compounds
china-s-november-2026-extraterritorial-r.summary: The October 2025 rare-earth controls suspended extraterritorial enforcement until November 10, 2026 — now only ~19 weeks away. CSIS data show US yttrium imports remain near historic lows, European approval rates for controlled-compound licenses are below 25%, and China's MIIT drafted new domestic production penalties in April 2026 that tighten the whole supply chain.

proposed_indicator.label: Nov-2026 extraterritorial enforcement countdown (weeks to activation / 0 extended / 1 on track to activate / 2 activated)
proposed_indicator.rubric: 0 = suspension extended again by diplomatic deal; 1 = on track to activate Nov 10, 2026 with no new deal (current); 2 = activated early or de-facto activated via aggressive enforcement actions before deadline. Score 1 currently with ~19 weeks remaining and no credible suspension deal in sight.

china-s-november-2026-extraterritorial-r.rationale: The existing s6_rare_earths tracker has an indicator 'Nov-2025 pause status' that is now stale — the Nov-2025 pause was implemented and the relevant live risk is whether the Nov-2026 extraterritorial enforcement date (which extends Chinese regulatory jurisdiction to any product containing ≥0.1% Chinese-origin rare earths, globally) will activate. No current indicator captures this countdown clock. With the Iran war consuming diplomatic bandwidth, a new deal to extend the pause looks unlikely, making this a near-term binary event risk.

genius-act-full-rulemaking-deadline-of-j.headline: GENIUS Act full-rulemaking deadline of July 18, 2026 approaches; Treasury/FinCEN AML proposed rule issued April 2026 but licensing, capital, and reserve regulations remain in proposed/comment stage
genius-act-full-rulemaking-deadline-of-j.summary: The GENIUS Act (enacted July 18, 2025) mandated that all primary regulations — covering licensing, capital, liquidity, and reserve standards for permitted payment stablecoin issuers — be promulgated by July 18, 2026. With the deadline two weeks away, the only issued rule is a FinCEN/OFAC AML proposed rule from April 2026; core prudential regulations are not yet final, creating a legal gap for the ~$250bn stablecoin market.
genius-act-full-rulemaking-deadline-of-j.target_indicator: GENIUS implementation friction (0 smooth / 1 gaps / 2 problems)
genius-act-full-rulemaking-deadline-of-j.rationale: The July 18, 2026 statutory rulemaking deadline for core GENIUS Act prudential regulations (licensing, capital, reserve standards) is 15 days away and core rules are not yet finalised — only the AML proposed rule has been issued (April 2026). This moves the GENIUS implementation friction indicator from '0 smooth' to '1 gaps': permitted issuers cannot fully comply with a framework whose licensing and reserve regulations do not yet exist in final form, creating legal uncertainty for a ~$250bn market that is directly connected to Treasury-bill demand (the s7_stablecoins story's core mechanism).

iran-war-energy-shock-drives-hormuz-fert.headline: Iran war energy shock drives Hormuz fertilizer disruption; Gulf fertilizer exports (especially Qatar LNG-linked urea) severely curtailed since March 2026
iran-war-energy-shock-drives-hormuz-fert.summary: The Strait of Hormuz crisis — still contested as of July 8 — has disrupted roughly 30% of globally traded fertilizers that normally transit the strait, including Gulf urea and ammonia exports, while Qatar LNG liquefaction was shut for weeks. The attack re-escalation on July 7 prolongs this food-input shock just as Northern Hemisphere crop seasons enter critical phases.
iran-war-energy-shock-drives-hormuz-fert.target_indicator: Hormuz/Gulf disruption to fertilizer inputs (0 clear / 1 disrupted / 2 cut)
iran-war-energy-shock-drives-hormuz-fert.rationale: The Wikipedia/CRS data confirms the Strait carries ~30% of globally traded fertilizers and that Gulf LNG/ammonia/urea have been severely disrupted since late February. The July 7 re-escalation moves the indicator back toward 2 (cut) after it may have been edging toward 1 (disrupted) during the ceasefire period. This is a lagged but accelerating food-security and EM-debt risk (linking s18 to s4_dev_debt) that the tracker needs to reflect as materially worsened.

japan-s-energy-import-crisis-deepens-as-.headline: Japan's energy import crisis deepens as Hormuz closure compounds BOJ policy bind — new macro feedback loop not captured in existing indicators
japan-s-energy-import-crisis-deepens-as-.summary: Japan sources ~90% of its oil from Middle East suppliers transiting Hormuz; the strait's near-closure since March 2026 has created an acute energy import shock on top of the yen collapse, pushing Japanese inflation higher and leaving the BOJ caught between tightening (to defend the yen) and easing (to offset the energy recession). This interaction between s2_japan and s20_sea_lanes is a structural feedback not tracked by any existing indicator.

proposed_indicator.label: Japan energy-import stress from Hormuz (0 normal / 1 elevated cost / 2 acute shortage)
proposed_indicator.rubric: 0 = Hormuz fully open, Japan energy imports at normal volumes and prices; 1 = Hormuz contested, Japan paying significant premium or sourcing alternatives at higher cost, BOJ policy complicated by stagflationary impulse (CURRENT — Japan relies on ~90% Middle East oil, Hormuz near-closed since March 2026, 10yr JGB at 2.70%, 30yr at 4.04%, yen at 40yr low); 2 = acute shortage, rationing, or emergency IEA drawdown triggered for Japan specifically.

japan-s-energy-import-crisis-deepens-as-.rationale: The s2_japan story tracks fiscal/JGB/carry risk in isolation, but a material new dimension has emerged: Japan's 90% dependence on Middle Eastern oil transiting Hormuz means the Iran war directly and severely worsens Japan's import bill, amplifies yen weakness (more USD needed for oil), raises domestic inflation (complicating BOJ), and increases the risk of a disorderly policy response. This cross-story feedback — Hormuz shock feeding into JGB/yen instability — is not captured by any existing s2 or s20 indicator and represents a systemic amplification channel that could accelerate the carry-trade unwind scenario. Score currently: 1 (elevated cost, no acute shortage yet due to IEA releases and Saudi pipeline diversions).

boj-hikes-to-1-signals-path-to-2-neutral.headline: BOJ hikes to 1%, signals path to 2% neutral — yen still hits 4-decade low, 10y JGB clears 2.7%
boj-hikes-to-1-signals-path-to-2-neutral.summary: The Bank of Japan raised its policy rate to 1% in late June 2026 with Deputy Governor Himino citing upside inflation risks from Middle East energy pass-through, yet USD/JPY has collapsed to four-decade lows because US rate expectations remain elevated and carry trades persist. The 10-year JGB yield climbed above 2.7% on July 2, its highest in three weeks, as markets price further BOJ hikes even as yen weakness deepens the stagflationary squeeze.
boj-hikes-to-1-signals-path-to-2-neutral.rationale: USD/JPY at four-decade lows (extreme risk-on reading for this indicator) combined with 10y JGB at 2.7%+ directly updates s2_japan's two primary market indicators. BOJ hiking to 1% while yen still collapses signals carry-trade stress and potential disorderly unwind — a core tail risk for this story. The 30y JGB yield and Takaichi financing-clarity indicators should also be re-checked given BOJ's aggressive tightening signal.

us-iran-mou-signed-june-19-hormuz-nomina.headline: US-Iran MOU signed June 19: Hormuz nominally re-opened, but full oil-flow restoration may take months and 60-day nuclear clock is running
us-iran-mou-signed-june-19-hormuz-nomina.summary: A 14-point US-Iran memorandum of understanding signed ~June 19 in Geneva commits Iran to arrange safe Hormuz passage and ends the US naval blockade in exchange for sanctions relief discussions, but experts warn mine-sweeping alone will take weeks and the 60-day window to resolve Iran's enriched-uranium stockpile is extraordinarily compressed. If the nuclear talks collapse, Iran retains leverage to re-close the strait.
us-iran-mou-signed-june-19-hormuz-nomina.target_indicator: Hormuz/Red Sea status (0 open / 1 contested / 2 closed)
us-iran-mou-signed-june-19-hormuz-nomina.rationale: The Hormuz status indicator should move from 2 (closed) toward 1 (contested/transitional): the MOU commits to re-opening but physical clearance lags, the 60-day nuclear negotiation cliff could trigger re-closure, and Iran's negotiating position still includes claims over strait governance. WTI has dropped on deal optimism but remains elevated vs pre-war. This is the single highest-materiality geopolitical de-escalation of the past week and directly governs s20_sea_lanes and indirectly s18_food_fertilizer and s4_dev_debt.

china-s-nov-2026-extraterritorial-rare-e.headline: China's Nov-2026 extraterritorial rare-earth enforcement deadline is 4 months away; aerospace yttrium shortages already reported
china-s-nov-2026-extraterritorial-rare-e.summary: The November 2025 suspension of China's second-wave rare-earth export controls (covering samarium, gadolinium, lutetium and extraterritorial provisions) expires November 10, 2026, while CSIS data show US heavy rare-earth imports (e.g., yttrium) remain well below 2024 levels despite partial easing; European licensing approval rates have fallen below 25%.
china-s-nov-2026-extraterritorial-rare-e.target_indicator: Nov-2025 pause status (0 extended / 1 expiring / 2 lapsed)
china-s-nov-2026-extraterritorial-rare-e.rationale: The November 10, 2026 expiry of China's second-wave rare-earth control pause is now within the 4-month horizon and should be moved from status 0 (extended) toward 1 (expiring). US heavy rare-earth imports remain structurally below 2024 levels, EU licensing approval rates below 25%, and China's March 2026 State Council Order No. 834 (supply-chain security framework) creates a new escalation layer. The tracker's Nov-2025 pause status indicator needs updating from 'extended' to 'expiring' to reflect the approaching cliff.

treasury-fincen-propose-genius-act-aml-s.headline: Treasury/FinCEN propose GENIUS Act AML/sanctions rule as July 18 implementation deadline approaches — rulemaking gap risk
treasury-fincen-propose-genius-act-aml-s.summary: On approximately July 2-3 2026, Treasury's FinCEN and OFAC issued a joint proposed rule implementing GENIUS Act anti-money laundering and sanctions compliance requirements for permitted payment stablecoin issuers, with the statute's one-year rulemaking deadline falling July 18, 2026. The proposed rule, still in notice-and-comment phase, means key implementation regulations will not be final by the statutory deadline, creating a compliance gap for issuers.
treasury-fincen-propose-genius-act-aml-s.target_indicator: GENIUS implementation friction (0 smooth / 1 gaps / 2 problems)
treasury-fincen-propose-genius-act-aml-s.rationale: GENIUS Act was signed July 18 2025; regulations were due July 18 2026 but FinCEN/OFAC only issued a proposed (not final) rule in the final days before deadline. This confirms a 'gaps' reading (score 1) on the implementation friction indicator: issuers face regulatory uncertainty on AML/sanctions compliance exactly as stablecoin supply continues to expand rapidly, creating systemic risk to Treasury-demand dynamics if a large issuer faces a compliance event.

boj-rate-hikes-failing-to-stem-yen-colla.headline: BOJ rate hikes failing to stem yen collapse: paradox creates carry-unwind cliff with no existing indicator capturing hike-vs-FX divergence
boj-rate-hikes-failing-to-stem-yen-colla.summary: Despite BOJ hiking to 1% and Board Member Tamura signaling a path to 2% 'at intervals of a few months', USD/JPY has sunk to four-decade lows as US rate expectations remain elevated — creating a toxic combination where tighter BOJ policy squeezes domestic balance sheets while the carry trade continues to fund leveraged positions in US assets. No current s2_japan indicator captures the BOJ-hike-pace vs yen-depreciation divergence that would trigger a sudden carry unwind.

proposed_indicator.label: BOJ policy rate vs USD/JPY divergence index (hike-pace adequacy)
proposed_indicator.rubric: 0 = BOJ hikes keeping pace with yen depreciation pressure (USD/JPY stable or strengthening yen at <145); 1 = BOJ hiking but yen still weakening (USD/JPY 145-160, carry trade persisting despite hikes); 2 = BOJ hiking aggressively but yen at multi-decade lows (USD/JPY >160), signalling imminent forced carry unwind or intervention. Current reading: 2.

boj-rate-hikes-failing-to-stem-yen-colla.rationale: The existing s2_japan indicators track USD/JPY level and JGB yields independently, but do not capture the dangerous divergence between the BOJ's hiking cycle and ongoing yen weakness — the very configuration that in August 2024 triggered the global carry-unwind shock. A composite divergence indicator would give early warning of the threshold where Japanese institutional investors (life insurers, pension funds) are forced to repatriate foreign assets, amplifying global bond and equity dislocations.

iran-mou-60-day-nuclear-clock-failure-sc.headline: Iran MOU 60-day nuclear clock: failure scenario could re-close Hormuz and create a new macro regime not covered by any existing story
iran-mou-60-day-nuclear-clock-failure-sc.summary: The June 19 MOU between the US and Iran gives only 60 days to resolve Iran's highly enriched uranium stockpile and nuclear program — issues that took years to negotiate in the 2015 JCPOA. If the 60-day window expires without agreement (mid-August 2026), Iran retains the legal and military option to re-close Hormuz, which would re-trigger a global energy crisis with second-order inflation, EM debt stress, and food-price spikes that cross-cut at least six existing tracker stories simultaneously.

proposed_story.name: Iran MOU nuclear-clock / Hormuz re-closure cliff
proposed_story.why: The June 19, 2026 US-Iran memorandum of understanding opens a 60-day window (expiring ~mid-August 2026) to negotiate Iran's nuclear program, sanctions relief, and Hormuz governance. Historical precedent (JCPOA negotiations took 2+ years) and current divergences (Israel not party; Iran demanding sovereignty over Hormuz; GCC demanding missile limits) make breakdown a credible base-case risk. A Hormuz re-closure would simultaneously spike WTI, reignite EM sovereign spreads, worsen Japan's energy import bill, increase food/fertilizer costs, and accelerate de-dollarization pressures — cross-cutting s4, s18, s20, s2, s12, and s5. No existing story captures the specific 60-day diplomatic countdown or the re-closure scenario as a discrete, time-bounded risk.

indicators.label: Iran-MOU negotiation status (0 = on track / 1 = stalled / 2 = collapsed)
indicators.rubric: 0 = substantive nuclear talks progressing, confidence-building measures being implemented; 1 = talks stalled, deadlines missed, US-Iran rhetorical escalation; 2 = MOU formally collapsed or Iran announces resumption of enrichment / closure of Hormuz. Current: 0 (just signed, clock started).

indicators.label: Days remaining in 60-day MOU window
indicators.rubric: Countdown from June 19 signing. Below 30 days with status=1 triggers escalation watch; zero days with status=1 or 2 triggers crisis protocol.

indicators.label: Hormuz physical re-opening completeness (0 = fully clear / 1 = partially operational / 2 = effectively closed)
indicators.rubric: Tracks mine-clearing progress, insurance-market re-opening of war-risk cover, and actual tanker traffic resumption. Current: 1 (MOU signed, physical clearance ongoing, insurance market cautious).

indicators.label: WTI crude oil (USD/bbl) — re-closure scenario proxy
indicators.rubric: Tracks energy market pricing of re-closure risk. Spike above $100 on MOU breakdown fears = escalation signal; sustained above $120 = crisis.

iran-mou-60-day-nuclear-clock-failure-sc.rationale: The Iran MOU nuclear 60-day clock is a discrete, time-bounded, high-consequence risk that no existing tracker story explicitly monitors. s20_sea_lanes tracks current Hormuz status, but not the forward diplomatic cliff that could flip it back to 'closed' within weeks. Given cross-story contagion (energy→inflation→EM debt→Japan stagflation→food prices→de-dollarization), this deserves a standalone story at level 7 with active countdown monitoring through mid-August 2026.

japan-30-yr-jgb-hits-4-04-and-yen-slides.headline: Japan 30-yr JGB hits 4.04% and yen slides to 4-decade low on July 2 — BOJ governance split deepens
japan-30-yr-jgb-hits-4-04-and-yen-slides.summary: On July 2, 2026, Japan's 30-year JGB yield rose to 4.04% and the 10-year climbed above 2.7%; the yen fell to a four-decade low driven by carry-trade persistence and wide rate differentials; PM Takaichi simultaneously appointed dovish new BOJ board member Ayano Sato (signalling weak inflation expectations), directly contradicting Governor Ueda's reiterated hawkish guidance and raising fiscal-monetary coordination risk.

proposed_indicator.label: BOJ internal governance friction (0 unified / 1 mixed signals / 2 overt split)
proposed_indicator.rubric: 0 = BOJ board and PM office aligned on policy direction; 1 = newly appointed board members sending conflicting signals vs governor (current: Sato appointment vs Ueda hawkish guidance); 2 = public board dissent or PM directly overriding BOJ independence

japan-30-yr-jgb-hits-4-04-and-yen-slides.rationale: The existing s2_japan indicators (USD/JPY, 30-yr JGB yield, Takaichi plan financing clarity) do not capture the new specific risk of a BOJ board governance split — Takaichi appointing a dovish member (Sato) while Ueda maintains a tightening bias creates a credibility-destroying internal contradiction that could trigger disorderly JGB repricing. This is a new, material sub-risk within the story that needs its own indicator. The 30-yr JGB at 4.04% and 10-yr at 2.7% also need immediate numerical updates.

global-lng-market-faces-structural-120-b.headline: Global LNG market faces structural 120 bcm cumulative supply loss 2026-2030 from Qatar infrastructure damage
global-lng-market-faces-structural-120-b.summary: IEA's April 2026 Gas Market Report quantified that damage to Qatari LNG liquefaction infrastructure at Ras Laffan — where two of 14 trains were damaged — will cause a cumulative loss of approximately 120 billion cubic meters of LNG supply between 2026 and 2030, permanently delaying the anticipated global LNG supply wave and reshaping European and Asian energy security for years.

proposed_story.name: LNG Infrastructure Destruction & Multi-Year Supply Gap
proposed_story.why: The Ras Laffan damage is not a temporary Hormuz-transit disruption (already captured in s20_sea_lanes) but a durable destruction of physical LNG liquefaction capacity. A 120 bcm cumulative loss 2026-2030 means the pre-war global LNG supply wave — which was expected to ease European and Asian energy prices — is structurally delayed. This creates multi-year energy inflation, locks Europe into Russian and US LNG dependency at high cost, pressures sovereign balances of Asian importers, and alters the economics of power-intensive AI data centers and electrification investment globally. No existing story captures permanent LNG infrastructure destruction as distinct from sea-lane access.

indicators.label: Qatar Ras Laffan LNG capacity restoration (% of pre-war nameplate online)
indicators.rubric: Target: 100% = pre-war; currently ~86% (2 of 14 trains offline); below 75% = acute; 75-90% = impaired; 90-100% = recovering. Sources: IEA Gas Market Report, IEEFA LNG Tracker.

indicators.label: Asian LNG spot price premium over TTF ($/MMBtu)
indicators.rubric: Measures demand-side stress on Asian importers; >$3 = elevated; >$6 = acute; negative = surplus. Tracks whether LNG shortage is pricing vulnerable economies (Pakistan, Bangladesh, Vietnam) out of the market.

indicators.label: Global LNG supply offline share (% of 2025 export capacity)
indicators.rubric: IEA confirmed ~25% of global LNG offline in early April 2026 (Qatar + Australian Cyclone Narelle + Russian constraints); <10% = normal; 10-20% = elevated; >20% = crisis.

global-lng-market-faces-structural-120-b.rationale: Existing s20_sea_lanes captures the Hormuz/Red Sea status and WTI crude price but does not track the permanent or multi-year destruction of LNG production infrastructure — a structurally different risk horizon. The IEA's quantification of 120 bcm cumulative loss through 2030 is a new, material finding that implies sustained energy inflation, European strategic dependency shifts, and long-duration stress for Asian LNG-importing sovereigns. This warrants a dedicated new story node in Set 5 (physical resource chokepoints).

ustr-section-301-forced-labor-tariff-hea.headline: USTR Section 301 forced-labor tariff hearings begin July 7; Section 122 global 10% surcharge expires ~July 24 — multi-economy tariff cliff imminent
ustr-section-301-forced-labor-tariff-hea.summary: USTR proposed 10–12.5% forced-labor tariffs on 60 economies on June 2, 2026; public hearings start July 7 with the goal of finalizing duties before the Section 122 surcharge expires around July 24. China has threatened to resume rare-earth export restrictions if new Section 301 probes targeting it proceed, and the US-China Board of Trade comment window closes July 10.

proposed_indicator.label: US tariff-cliff transition risk (0 smooth / 1 friction / 2 cliff-edge)
proposed_indicator.rubric: 0 = Section 122 replaced cleanly by Section 301 with no gap or escalation; 1 = procedural delay or partial coverage gap causes uncertainty for importers; 2 = Section 122 lapses without replacement OR China retaliates with renewed rare-earth/soybean curbs in response to new Section 301 actions

ustr-section-301-forced-labor-tariff-hea.rationale: The existing s5_china_deflation story tracks tariff/anti-dumping actions, but the imminent July 24 tariff-cliff — where the Section 122 surcharge expires and must be replaced by Section 301 forced-labor duties covering 60 economies — is a distinct structural transition risk not captured by the current 'New tariff/anti-dumping actions' indicator. The hearing starts THIS WEEK (July 7) and China has explicitly threatened rare-earth retaliation if Section 301 probes expand, directly linking this to s6_rare_earths as well. A new indicator is needed for the tariff-cliff transition mechanics.

new-risk-iran-war-creates-a-sovereign-en.headline: New risk: Iran War creates a sovereign energy-credit shock for Gulf petrostates — fiscal buffers depleting, GCC dollar-peg credibility under stress
new-risk-iran-war-creates-a-sovereign-en.summary: All Persian Gulf nations (Saudi Arabia, UAE, Iraq, Qatar, Kuwait) have experienced sharply lower oil export revenues since the Hormuz disruption began February 28, 2026, even though global oil prices rose. Saudi Aramco pipeline exports (Red Sea route) partially mitigate this, but Iraq, Qatar (LNG), and Kuwait face near-total export shutdown. GCC sovereign wealth fund drawdowns and dollar-peg defense costs are rising, with knock-on effects for EM dollar bond markets and petrodollar recycling into US Treasuries.

proposed_story.name: GCC petro-state fiscal stress & dollar-peg credibility
proposed_story.why: The 2026 Iran war has produced a paradox where global oil prices rose sharply but Gulf producer export revenues collapsed due to Hormuz closure. Iraq, Qatar, and Kuwait face near-zero LNG/crude export volumes. Saudi Arabia is partially insulated via the East-West pipeline but fiscal breakeven oil prices ($70–90/bbl for most GCC states) are at risk if the disruption persists into H2 2026. GCC sovereign wealth funds are drawing down reserves; petrodollar recycling into US Treasuries is declining at a time when the US fiscal deficit is widening. Dollar-peg credibility (especially Kuwaiti dinar, Qatari riyal) is under pressure. This is distinct from s4_dev_debt (which focuses on EM borrowers) and s20_sea_lanes (which focuses on shipping logistics) — it captures the fiscal-sovereign-credit-petrodollar nexus.

indicators.label: GCC export revenue loss vs. pre-conflict baseline (%, 3-month rolling avg)
indicators.rubric: Estimated % decline in aggregate GCC crude+LNG export revenues vs. Jan 2026 baseline, sourced from IEA/OPEC monthly oil market reports. Values above -30% = moderate stress; above -50% = severe; above -70% = crisis (triggers SWF liquidation pressure).

indicators.label: GCC dollar-peg stress (0 stable / 1 forward premium rising / 2 peg under attack)
indicators.rubric: 0 = USD/SAR, USD/QAR, USD/KWD 12-month NDF forwards within 0.5% of spot peg; 1 = NDF forwards showing >0.5% devaluation premium or CDS spreads widening >50bp; 2 = explicit peg defense operations, IMF consultation, or GCC central bank emergency liquidity action.

indicators.label: Petrodollar Treasury demand proxy (0 stable / 1 declining / 2 net selling)
indicators.rubric: 0 = Gulf-region custodial Treasury holdings (TIC data) flat or rising; 1 = month-over-month decline >$10bn for two consecutive months; 2 = net selling >$30bn/month or evidence of SWF reallocation away from USD assets.

new-risk-iran-war-creates-a-sovereign-en.rationale: No existing story captures the feedback loop between Gulf export revenue collapse → GCC SWF drawdown → reduced petrodollar recycling → US Treasury demand decline → term premium pressure. This is a second-order financial shock from the Iran War that operates through sovereign credit and dollar-peg channels rather than through shipping logistics (s20) or EM debt (s4). With the Hormuz ceasefire fragile and ADNOC projecting no full flow restoration until 2027, this stress is likely to persist and intensify through H2 2026, warranting a dedicated story at proposed_level 7.

china-widens-japan-dual-use-rare-earth-e.headline: China Widens Japan Dual-Use/Rare-Earth Entity Blacklist to 80 Named Firms Including Defense Research Institutes (June 29)
