china-widens-japan-dual-use-rare-earth-e.summary: Beijing added four Japanese government defense institutes and 36 additional commercial entities to its export control and watch lists on June 29, bringing total named entities to 80 since the January blanket ban. Rare earth shipments to Japan are already down 78% from pre-ban levels, and economists estimate a sustained one-year cutoff would reduce Japan's real GDP by ~1.3% (~¥7 trillion).
china-widens-japan-dual-use-rare-earth-e.rationale: The June 29 action is directly material to s6_rare_earths. The 'Rare-earth export license flow' indicator should be updated toward '2 = frozen' for Japan-destined flows (down 78%). The 'US domestic RE producer blacklist' indicator may also need a new dimension covering Japan-targeted entity bans. Additionally, the November 2025 pause status indicator (s6) should note that the broader US-China suspension expires November 2026, keeping expiry risk live. This is the clearest 7-day update to s6 and warrants a watch-level escalation.

yen-hits-40-year-low-near-160-usd-despit.headline: Yen Hits 40-Year Low Near 160/USD Despite $73bn Intervention; BOJ Hike Fails to Close Rate Gap
yen-hits-40-year-low-near-160-usd-despit.summary: Japan's yen remains pinned near ¥160/USD as of early July — four-decade lows — despite ¥11.7 trillion (~$73bn) in FX intervention from April–May and a BOJ rate hike to a three-decade high. A 300bp US-Japan rate differential keeps the carry trade structurally attractive, while the new dovish BOJ board member (Sato) signals further policy friction.

proposed_indicator.label: MoF FX intervention cumulative (¥ tn, rolling 12-mo)
proposed_indicator.rubric: 0 = <¥5tn (no sustained pressure) | 1 = ¥5–15tn (elevated, carry trade persistent) | 2 = >¥15tn or reserves visibly depleting (intervention fatigue / credibility loss). Current reading: ~¥11.7tn → score 1, trending toward 2.

yen-hits-40-year-low-near-160-usd-despit.rationale: s2_japan tracks USD/JPY and JGB yields but has no indicator for intervention exhaustion / reserve depletion risk. The ¥73bn deployment with zero durable effect is a qualitatively new dimension: Japan's foreign-reserve buffer is being consumed faster than the structural carry trade can be closed by monetary policy. Without a cumulative intervention indicator, the tracker will miss the inflection point where MoF runs low on dry powder — precisely the moment carry-unwind tail risk becomes systemic.

china-ppi-surges-to-3-9-yoy-in-may-3rd-c.headline: China PPI Surges to +3.9% YoY in May (3rd Consecutive Monthly Rise, Fastest Since July 2022) Driven by Iran-War Energy Spike
china-ppi-surges-to-3-9-yoy-in-may-3rd-c.summary: China's factory-gate prices accelerated sharply in May 2026 to +3.9% YoY, flipping the multi-year deflation narrative, as the Iran war-driven commodity shock lifted mining (+15.8% YoY) and raw-material costs. Consumer goods prices remain in mild deflation (-0.8%), creating an internal squeeze on downstream margins.
china-ppi-surges-to-3-9-yoy-in-may-3rd-c.rationale: s5_china_deflation's core indicator is China PPI YoY, and the swing from a multi-year deflation streak to +3.9% YoY is the most material recent update. The tracker's rubric flags 'large swing either way is worse' — this is now a cost-push shock transmitted outward rather than a deflation spillover, which changes the direction of the risk. The story's framing should be updated to reflect the inflation/margin-squeeze scenario rather than pure deflation. The indicator reading now sits at the high-risk end of the band.

china-japan-rare-earth-dual-use-controls.headline: China–Japan Rare-Earth / Dual-Use Controls Expand to Trigger GDP-Level Supply Chain Risk; November 2026 Broader Suspension Expiry Looms
china-japan-rare-earth-dual-use-controls.summary: With the Oct-2025 measures' extraterritorial enforcement suspended until November 10, 2026, the clock is running. US yttrium imports remain far below pre-restriction levels, and China's MIIT published a tighter domestic enforcement framework in April 2026 covering mining, smelting, and separation penalties — indicating Beijing is consolidating structural control regardless of diplomatic pauses.
china-japan-rare-earth-dual-use-controls.rationale: The Nov-2025 pause status indicator should be updated to 1 (expiring) — the November 10, 2026 extraterritorial enforcement deadline is now ~18 weeks away, US imports of controlled REEs remain structurally depressed, and Beijing's April 2026 domestic enforcement tightening signals the pause is being used as a compliance-preparation window rather than a genuine détente. Failure to negotiate a renewal before November would activate FDPR-style extraterritorial controls across global supply chains, a step-change for s6 severity.

strait-of-hormuz-60-day-mou-signed-june-.headline: Strait of Hormuz 60-day MOU signed June 17; Iran still contesting toll-free passage as of July 1–3
strait-of-hormuz-60-day-mou-signed-june-.summary: The US and Iran signed a 60-day memorandum of understanding on June 17, 2026 to end hostilities, with Hormuz reopening a core condition; however Iran is demanding sovereign toll authority over the strait and the US Navy announced a widened Oman-side route on June 27, signalling the corridor remains operationally contested. WTI has retreated from ~$89 in late May to the low-$70s by July 1 but the MOU is fragile and unimplemented.

proposed_indicator.label: Hormuz_toll_sovereignty_risk
proposed_indicator.rubric: 0 = toll-free transit confirmed and implemented; 1 = disputed / MOU signed but not implemented / Iran asserting toll rights; 2 = tolls enforced or strait re-closed

strait-of-hormuz-60-day-mou-signed-june-.rationale: The existing s20 indicator 'Hormuz/Red Sea status' uses a 0-open/1-contested/2-closed rubric, which currently reads '1-contested.' However the qualitatively new development is Iran's explicit demand for sovereign toll authority — a structural change to the strait's legal regime that would persist even after hostilities end and is not captured by any current indicator. The June 27 US Navy widened-route manoeuvre signals Washington is also not accepting Iranian control, keeping the corridor in an unstable equilibrium that materially affects oil (s20), food/fertilizer inputs (s18), and global inflation (s4/s2). A dedicated toll-sovereignty risk indicator is needed.

yen-hits-four-decade-low-10y-jgb-above-2.headline: Yen hits four-decade low, 10y JGB above 2.70%; BOJ July 30-31 meeting now live for rate hike
yen-hits-four-decade-low-10y-jgb-above-2.summary: Japan's 10-year JGB yield climbed above 2.70% in the week of June 30–July 3 — its highest since the post-YCC exit — as the yen slid to its weakest level in four decades on wide US-Japan rate differentials and persistent carry-trade pressure. BOJ Governor Ueda reiterated readiness to hike further, and the July 30-31 MPM is now a live event, with a newly appointed dovish board member (Ayano Sato) adding uncertainty to the vote.
yen-hits-four-decade-low-10y-jgb-above-2.rationale: The yen at a four-decade low and the 10y JGB pushing above 2.70% directly update the tracker's primary s2 indicators. Oxford Economics has revised its end-2026 10y JGB forecast to 2.80% from 2.50%, citing Takaichi's fiscal expansion. The July 30 BOJ meeting is a near-term catalyst for carry-trade unwind risk — the same mechanism that triggered the August 2024 global volatility episode. The Takaichi plan financing clarity indicator should move to '1 unclear' given expanding fiscal promises (VAT cuts on food, supplementary budgets) against BOJ QT. High materiality because a surprise BOJ hike or yen snap-back could transmit globally via leveraged carry positions.

genius-act-implementing-regulations-miss.headline: GENIUS Act implementing regulations missed the July 18, 2026 statutory deadline; effective date now defaults to January 2027
genius-act-implementing-regulations-miss.summary: The GENIUS Act (signed July 18, 2025) required all primary federal stablecoin regulators to issue final rules within one year (by July 18, 2026). As of July 13, the Federal Reserve Board — regulator for PPSI subsidiaries of state member banks — has not issued even a proposed rule, creating a structural gap that prevents the framework from being 'complete and coherent' and could delay the law's effective date or leave a regulatory limbo for a large class of bank-affiliated stablecoin issuers.
genius-act-implementing-regulations-miss.rationale: The GENIUS implementation friction indicator (0 smooth / 1 gaps / 2 problems) should move from 0 to 1 (gaps). The Federal Reserve Board — the regulator of the largest class of bank-affiliated stablecoin subsidiaries — has not published even a proposed rule ahead of the statutory July 18 deadline. Six other agencies have published NPRMs, but the Fed's absence creates a structural gap that could force state-member-bank PPSIs into regulatory limbo or delay the law's effective date beyond January 18, 2027. This is material to the Treasury-demand angle because the 1:1 reserve rule requires stablecoin growth to translate into T-bill demand, which depends on the framework becoming operative.

china-s-nov-10-2026-rare-earth-extraterr.headline: China's Nov 10, 2026 rare-earth extraterritorial enforcement cliff is now less than 130 days away, with no successor agreement in sight
china-s-nov-10-2026-rare-earth-extraterr.summary: The suspended October 2025 rare-earth export controls — covering extraterritorial FDPR-style reach over any product containing ≥0.1% Chinese-origin REEs — expire on November 10, 2026. No formal extension or successor deal has been announced, and US rare-earth imports of key elements (e.g., yttrium) remain well below pre-restriction levels despite the partial Nov-2025 easing, with export flows continuing to favour Europe over the US.

proposed_indicator.label: Nov2026_extraterritorial_cliff_days_remaining
proposed_indicator.rubric: Days until November 10, 2026 enforcement date for suspended Oct-2025 extraterritorial controls; track alongside diplomatic signalling: >180d = low urgency; 90-180d = elevated; <90d without successor deal = critical

china-s-nov-10-2026-rare-earth-extraterr.rationale: The existing s6 tracker monitors the 'Nov-2025 pause status' (now extended to Nov-2026) but has no indicator for the approaching cliff on the suspended October 2025 extraterritorial measures — the most consequential layer, which would impose Chinese regulatory jurisdiction on global supply chains. With <130 days to the deadline and no successor framework, this is transitioning from a background risk to an acute event risk. The January 2026 Catalogue expansion (samarium, gadolinium, lutetium) already added new controls, and China's March 2026 State Council Order 834 created a unified supply-chain security mandate. US rare-earth imports of yttrium are still 95% below pre-restriction levels (17 vs 333 tons over comparable 8-month periods), making this operationally material for defense, EV, and semiconductor supply chains.

us-cpi-at-4-2-yoy-may-2026-new-fed-chair.headline: US CPI at 4.2% YoY (May 2026); new Fed Chair Warsh eliminates forward guidance and holds rates at 3.50-3.75%
us-cpi-at-4-2-yoy-may-2026-new-fed-chair.summary: US headline CPI reached 4.2% year-on-year in May 2026 — the highest since April 2023 — driven by energy pass-through from the Hormuz disruption and sticky services inflation. New Fed Chair Kevin Warsh removed traditional forward guidance after the June meeting, shifting to pure data-dependence, leaving markets without rate-path anchors at a moment of elevated inflation and geopolitical uncertainty.

proposed_story.name: US stagflation / Fed credibility regime shift
proposed_story.why: The combination of 4.2% CPI (a 3-year high), a Fed holding rates at 3.50-3.75% under a new Chair who has abandoned forward guidance, and an economy still absorbing Hormuz-shock energy costs creates a qualitatively new macro regime: policy uncertainty is elevated precisely when inflation is resurgent. This is not captured by any existing story — s2 covers Japan/BOJ, s4 covers EM debt, s11 covers consumer debt — but the US-specific stagflation / Fed credibility risk thread (higher-for-longer forcing EM capital flight, re-pricing risk assets, and stressing floating-rate private credit and CRE simultaneously) is a cross-cutting amplifier of existing stories that deserves its own tracker node.

indicators.label: US_CPI_YoY_%
indicators.rubric: Monthly US BLS headline CPI YoY; <3% = contained; 3-4% = elevated; >4% = resurgent / tracker alert

indicators.label: Fed_forward_guidance_clarity
indicators.rubric: 0 = explicit dot-plot / forward guidance in place; 1 = data-dependent / guidance removed; 2 = active policy reversal signal (hike threat or surprise cut)

indicators.label: US_real_policy_rate_%
indicators.rubric: EFFR midpoint minus 12m trailing CPI; positive = restrictive; negative = accommodative despite high inflation (stagflation warning)

us-cpi-at-4-2-yoy-may-2026-new-fed-chair.rationale: No existing story in the tracker explicitly owns the US macro regime — the closest proxies are s1 (Treasury basis/SOFR), s3 (private credit), and s11 (consumer debt), but none tracks the primary driver: a new-Fed-Chair credibility shock coinciding with the highest inflation in 3 years. With CPI at 4.2% and the Fed funds rate at 3.50-3.75%, the real policy rate is modestly negative, meaning monetary policy remains de facto accommodative even as Chair Warsh signals data-dependence. This is a standalone systemic risk because it (a) raises the probability of a surprise hike that would detonate leveraged positions in s1/s8/s9, (b) entrenches dollar strength that stresses s4 EM debt, and (c) erodes the consumer disposable income underpinning s11.

obbba-signed-july-4-2025-cbo-scores-2-8-.headline: OBBBA signed July 4 2025: CBO scores $2.8–3.4T deficit addition, $5T debt ceiling hike; US now downgraded by all three major rating agencies
obbba-signed-july-4-2025-cbo-scores-2-8-.summary: The One Big Beautiful Bill Act — now law for one year — adds an estimated $2.8–3.4 trillion to the US national debt over the next decade (over $4T with interest), raised the debt ceiling by $5 trillion to $41.1T, and has contributed to the US being downgraded by S&P, Moody's, and Fitch. Yale Budget Lab projects 10-year Treasury yields 1.2 percentage points higher by 2054 under this trajectory, amplifying the term-premium feedback loop on global sovereign debt.

proposed_indicator.label: US sovereign fiscal stress score (0 contained / 1 deteriorating / 2 acute)
proposed_indicator.rubric: 0 = debt/GDP path stable and no major agency downgrade; 1 = CBO-scored deficit addition >$1T/decade or single major downgrade; 2 = all three agencies have downgraded and CBO scores >$2.5T deficit addition over decade with no credible offset plan. Current reading: 2.

obbba-signed-july-4-2025-cbo-scores-2-8-.rationale: s4_dev_debt tracks emerging-market debt fragility and uses the broad dollar index and TIPS yields. The OBBBA's enacted fiscal deterioration is now a first-order input to global term premia, US 10y real yields, and dollar-reserve demand — all of which directly feed back into EM debt stress. However, no existing s4 indicator captures US sovereign fiscal credibility itself. The triple downgrade and $5T ceiling hike are new facts requiring a dedicated ordinal indicator. This is a story_gap rather than new_risk because the transmission channel (US real yields → dollar index → EM debt) is already in s4.

us-june-nfp-collapses-to-57k-vs-115k-con.headline: US June NFP collapses to 57K (vs 115K consensus); unemployment 4.2%; CPI still 4.2% YoY — stagflationary squeeze tightens on consumer balance sheets
us-june-nfp-collapses-to-57k-vs-115k-con.summary: June 2026 nonfarm payrolls of just 57,000 — less than half the prior month's 129,000 and well below expectations — confirm a rapid deterioration in the US labor market even as CPI inflation remains at 4.2% year-on-year, the highest since April 2023. The stagflationary dynamic increases pressure on subprime consumer segments, BNPL borrowers, and auto-loan delinquencies that the tracker monitors under s11.
us-june-nfp-collapses-to-57k-vs-115k-con.rationale: Unemployment ticking up to 4.2% (from ~3.9% at year-start) while CPI stays at 4.2% is the classic stagflationary pinch that pressures consumer debt quality. The s11 tracker monitors this via the unemployment rate sub-indicator and BNPL delinquency trend. 57K payrolls is also a leading indicator of worsening subprime auto and credit-card performance 1–2 quarters forward, making this a material update even if unemployment is only 4.2% today. The Fed's removal of forward guidance under new Chair Warsh and the 3.50–3.75% FFR also leaves the policy response path unclear, amplifying the consumer stress risk.

war-risk-insurance-market-dislocation-el.headline: War-risk insurance market dislocation: elevated Hormuz premiums and mine risk mean physical oil-market normalization may lag ceasefire politics by 12–18 months
war-risk-insurance-market-dislocation-el.summary: Even after the June 17 MOU, shipping companies report that war-risk insurance premiums for Hormuz transit remain prohibitively high, sea mines have not been fully cleared, and many operators are switching off AIS transponders. The UAE's ADNOC CEO stated full flows through Hormuz will not resume until 2027 even under a speedy deal, creating a structural insurance-market dislocation that crosses the s20 and s13 story boundaries but is not currently captured by any indicator.

proposed_indicator.label: Marine war-risk insurance dislocation (0 normal / 1 elevated premium / 2 market withdrawal)
proposed_indicator.rubric: 0 = war-risk insurance freely available at near-normal pricing for major chokepoints; 1 = premiums materially elevated (>3x peacetime) or leading P&I clubs issuing voyage exclusions for named regions; 2 = market effectively withdrawing from named chokepoints, government backstop required, >50% of pre-conflict vessel traffic avoiding the zone. Current reading: 1–2 transition for Hormuz.

war-risk-insurance-market-dislocation-el.rationale: s13_insurance tracks insurer non-renewals and catastrophe-loss pressure, focused on property/casualty and mortgage insurance retreat. Marine war-risk insurance dislocation is a distinct but related failure mode: when P&I clubs and Lloyd's syndicates effectively price shipping out of a major chokepoint, physical oil supply cannot normalize even after a political ceasefire. This is a material gap because: (1) it persists independent of the political MOU; (2) it feeds directly into s20 Hormuz status, s18 food/fertilizer inputs, and global inflation via energy costs; and (3) government backstop decisions (like the UK/France defensive mission) are partly insurance-driven. The indicator should live in s13 but be cross-referenced in s20.

section-122-universal-tariff-expires-jul.headline: Section 122 universal tariff expires July 24 with no confirmed replacement; Section 301 investigations racing to conclude
section-122-universal-tariff-expires-jul.summary: The 15% Section 122 blanket import surcharge (imposed after the Supreme Court struck down IEEPA tariffs in February 2026, itself also judicially invalidated by the CIT in May) expires July 24, 2026; the administration is rushing Section 301 and Section 232 investigations to plug the gap before expiry, creating a high-uncertainty cliff for global trade and supply chains.

proposed_story.name: US tariff-regime legal cliff & WTO/supply-chain cascade
proposed_story.why: The US has lost two consecutive statutory bases for broad tariffs (IEEPA struck down Feb 2026 by SCOTUS; Section 122 struck down May 2026 by CIT, currently stayed on appeal) and Section 122 expires July 24. The administration is racing to substitute Section 301/232 authority. A disorderly transition or gap would abruptly cut effective tariff rates (deflationary shock to import-competing sectors, dollar-negative) or trigger retaliatory escalation if Section 301 findings land hard. This legal/structural tariff instability is not captured by any existing story.

indicators.label: Section 122 / successor tariff coverage (% of US imports subject to ≥10% surcharge)
indicators.rubric: ≥80% = status quo; 50-79% = gap/partial lapse; <50% = significant cliff/lapse

indicators.label: US tariff legal status (0 = stable authority / 1 = court challenge pending / 2 = struck down & gap)
indicators.rubric: 0 = Supreme Court-affirmed authority in place; 1 = active appellate litigation with stay; 2 = injunction in effect with coverage gap

indicators.label: Section 301/232 replacement tariff enactment pace (0 = on track / 1 = delayed / 2 = gap materializing)
indicators.rubric: Track whether USTR Section 301 findings and new Section 232 proclamations are published before July 24 expiry

section-122-universal-tariff-expires-jul.rationale: No existing story covers the structural legal fragility of the US tariff regime. IEEPA was struck down by SCOTUS in February 2026, Section 122 was struck down by the CIT in May 2026 (though stayed), and Section 122 expires July 24 regardless. The administration is relying on Section 301/232 investigations concluding in time, which introduces a binary cliff event this week. A disorderly lapse would abruptly reduce average US tariff rates (deflationary / dollar-negative), while an aggressive Section 301 replacement could trigger WTO disputes and retaliation, both material for global macro.

genius-act-implementing-regulation-deadl.headline: GENIUS Act implementing-regulation deadline hits July 18, 2026; rulemaking still in proposed stages across OCC, FinCEN, and Fed
genius-act-implementing-regulation-deadl.summary: The GENIUS Act (signed July 18, 2025) requires final regulations from federal banking agencies by July 18, 2026; as of early July, the OCC, FinCEN/OFAC, and Federal Reserve rules remain in proposed/comment stages, creating a gap between the statutory deadline and actual implementation readiness.
genius-act-implementing-regulation-deadl.target_indicator: GENIUS implementation friction (0 smooth / 1 gaps / 2 problems)

proposed_indicator.label: GENIUS rulemaking completion rate (% of required final rules published by July 18, 2026 deadline)
proposed_indicator.rubric: 0 = all major rules finalized on time (OCC, FinCEN, Fed Reserve); 1 = 1-2 agencies missing deadline or rules in re-proposal; 2 = majority of rules missing, effective date uncertain or delayed beyond Jan 2027

genius-act-implementing-regulation-deadl.rationale: The existing GENIUS implementation friction indicator (0/1/2 band) is the right story home, but the current indicator does not capture the specific statutory deadline risk materializing this week. Missing the July 18 rulemaking deadline would push the Act's effective date toward the 18-month backstop (January 2027), delaying the stablecoin Treasury-demand channel and creating legal grey zones for OCC-chartered issuers (Circle, Paxos). This is a story_gap because the indicator exists but lacks a sub-indicator that tracks the rulemaking completion rate against the statutory clock.

new-fed-chair-warsh-drops-forward-guidan.headline: New Fed Chair Warsh drops forward guidance, holds rates at 3.50-3.75%; US CPI 4.2% YoY in May 2026 — highest since April 2023
new-fed-chair-warsh-drops-forward-guidan.summary: Fed Chair Kevin Warsh removed traditional forward guidance after the June FOMC meeting, shifting to pure data dependence at a 3.50-3.75% funds rate target, while May 2026 CPI printed 4.2% YoY — the highest in three years — driven partly by tariff pass-through and Middle East energy shocks, widening the policy uncertainty premium embedded in Treasury markets.

proposed_indicator.label: Fed policy-framework uncertainty score (0 = clear forward guidance / 1 = data-dependent with elevated inflation / 2 = framework shift with inflation >4% and no guidance)
proposed_indicator.rubric: 0 = Fed provides explicit rate-path guidance with inflation near target; 1 = data-dependent language, inflation 2-4%, markets can model distribution; 2 = explicit removal of forward guidance combined with CPI >4% and new chair regime change — maximum model uncertainty, basis-trade leverage risk elevated

new-fed-chair-warsh-drops-forward-guidan.rationale: The Warsh Fed's explicit removal of forward guidance combined with CPI at a 3-year high (4.2%) is a structural change in the information environment for Treasury basis traders. Basis trades are highly sensitive to yield volatility regime changes; without forward guidance, the distribution of future rate outcomes widens, increasing the probability of sudden MOVE spikes that can force basis-trade unwinds. The MOVE index indicator captures the symptom but not this new causal driver. A story_gap indicator on Fed framework uncertainty is warranted under s1_basis_trade, as no existing indicator tracks the guidance-removal risk.

section-301-pharmaceutical-and-semicondu.headline: Section 301 pharmaceutical and semiconductor tariffs imminent: July 31 and Sept 29 effective dates set via April 2 executive order
section-301-pharmaceutical-and-semicondu.summary: A Trump executive order issued April 2, 2026 set pharmaceutical/medical device Section 232 tariffs to take effect July 31, 2026, with a second tranche on September 29, adding to existing 25% semiconductor tariffs; the administration intends these to replace the expiring Section 122 surcharge and extend tariff pressure on goods currently exempt from other authority.
section-301-pharmaceutical-and-semicondu.target_indicator: New tariff/anti-dumping actions (0 calm / 1 rising / 2 escalating)

proposed_indicator.label: US sector-specific Section 232/301 tariff cascade index (0 = stable / 1 = new sector added / 2 = multi-sector cascade with pharmaceutical + semiconductor + auto active simultaneously)
proposed_indicator.rubric: 0 = no new sector-specific 232/301 tariffs in prior 90 days; 1 = one major sector newly tariffed (e.g., pharmaceuticals July 31); 2 = three or more major sectors newly tariffed within 90 days, covering >$500bn in annual import value

section-301-pharmaceutical-and-semicondu.rationale: Pharmaceutical tariffs (July 31) will directly raise input costs for global supply chains and could push already-elevated US CPI higher, exacerbating China's deflationary export pressure as Chinese manufacturers absorb cost-push. The existing s5_china_deflation 'new tariff/anti-dumping actions' indicator is the right home but currently captures aggregate tone; a more granular sector-cascade sub-indicator would allow the tracker to distinguish between broad blanket tariffs and targeted sector escalations that affect PPI and deflationary dynamics differently.

us-iran-hormuz-mou-holding-but-fragile-s.headline: US-Iran Hormuz MOU Holding But Fragile: Strait Partially Reopening, Oil Price Collapsing
us-iran-hormuz-mou-holding-but-fragile-s.summary: On July 7–8, three commercial ships were struck in the Strait of Hormuz; the US launched retaliatory strikes and President Trump declared the June 17 Islamabad Memorandum ceasefire finished, revoking Iran sanctions waivers. WTI crude rose from ~$69 to ~$75/bbl and Brent to ~$79 within 48 hours, with the Hormuz chokepoint (20% of global seaborne oil) reverting to 'contested' status after having partially reopened post-ceasefire.
us-iran-hormuz-mou-holding-but-fragile-s.target_indicator: Hormuz/Red Sea status (0 open / 1 contested / 2 closed) AND WTI crude oil (USD/bbl, CL=F)
us-iran-hormuz-mou-holding-but-fragile-s.rationale: The Hormuz status indicator should be moved from '1 contested' back toward '2 contested/near-closed' given Trump's ceasefire declaration ending and active US strikes. WTI crude updated to ~$75/bbl. This is the single most systemically important geopolitical event of the week, with direct cascading effects on inflation, central bank policy (s2_japan, s18_food_fertilizer), EM sovereign spreads (s4_dev_debt), and AI supply chains (s8_ai_capex, s19_lithography).

japan-10-year-jgb-yield-surges-to-2-7-2-.headline: Japan 10-Year JGB Yield Surges to ~2.7–2.8% (30-Year High), Yen Near 4-Decade Low; BOJ Hikes to 1%
japan-10-year-jgb-yield-surges-to-2-7-2-.summary: Japan's June 30 fiscal blueprint (mobilising ¥370 trillion through 2040) contained language calling for 'appropriate monetary policy' to support growth, sparking fears the government would rein in BOJ rate hikes. The 10-year JGB yield surged to 2.88% — a 30-year high — while USD/JPY ground near ¥162, close to 40-year yen lows, with long/super-long yields under sustained selling pressure.
japan-10-year-jgb-yield-surges-to-2-7-2-.target_indicator: 30-year JGB yield (%) AND Takaichi plan financing clarity (0 ok / 1 unclear / 2 disorderly)
japan-10-year-jgb-yield-surges-to-2-7-2-.rationale: The 10-year JGB yield at 2.88% (30-year high) directly updates the '30-year JGB yield' indicator. The government blueprint language pressuring the BOJ to slow hikes is exactly the 'Takaichi plan financing clarity' indicator — status has moved from 1 (unclear) toward 2 (disorderly) given market reaction. USD/JPY near ¥162 (40-year yen low) is simultaneously a carry-trade unwind risk signal and a BOJ-pressure amplifier, materially elevating the Japan fiscal/JGB/carry risk story.

section-301-tariff-cliff-july-24-expiry-.headline: Section 301 Tariff Cliff: July 24 Expiry of Section 122 Global Surcharge as USTR Hearings Conclude
section-301-tariff-cliff-july-24-expiry-.summary: USTR held public hearings July 7, 2026 on proposed new Section 301 tariffs (10–12.5%) covering 60 economies to replace the 10% Section 122 global surcharge expiring July 24; if the new tariffs are not finalized in time there is a brief potential tariff gap that would revert imports to pre-IEEPA duty rates, creating acute uncertainty for global supply chains. China warned it could reinstate rare-earth export restrictions if Section 301 probes proceed against it.

proposed_indicator.label: US tariff-regime transition risk (0 orderly / 1 gap / 2 reversion)
proposed_indicator.rubric: 0 = Section 301 tariffs finalized before July 24 Section 122 expiry with no coverage gap; 1 = brief gap or partial coverage lapse requiring executive patch; 2 = Section 122 lapses without replacement, broad tariff reversion to pre-IEEPA rates, or China retaliates with renewed rare-earth/trade restrictions in direct response to Section 301 action

section-301-tariff-cliff-july-24-expiry-.rationale: The July 24 Section 122 expiry is an acute, dated event that none of the current s5_china_deflation indicators (China PPI, USD/CNY, tariff escalation) fully capture. The new Section 301 action covers 60 economies—not just China—and represents the broadest use of Section 301 in history, with direct linkage to s6_rare_earths (China warned RE retaliation), s5_china_deflation (new tariff/anti-dumping actions), and s4_dev_debt (EM export disruption). Adding a tariff-transition indicator to s5 (which already tracks tariff/anti-dumping actions) plugs this gap with a time-bounded, measurable rubric for the next 3 weeks.

boj-s-new-board-appointee-signals-dovish.headline: BOJ's New Board Appointee Signals Dovish Lean; Internal Split Sharpens as Yen Hits 4-Decade Low
boj-s-new-board-appointee-signals-dovish.summary: Prime Minister Takaichi appointed Ayano Sato to the BOJ board who signaled inflation expectations remain 'relatively weak'—a more accommodative stance at odds with hawkish board members like Tamura who called for hikes every few months toward 2% neutral. This internal BOJ split, combined with a yen at a four-decade low driven by carry trade persistence and US-Japan rate differentials, creates a disorderly policy trilemma: hike to defend yen (risking fiscal blowup on ¥370tr plan) or hold (allowing yen depreciation to deepen inflation).

proposed_indicator.label: BOJ policy coherence / political independence (0 unified-hawkish / 1 split / 2 politically-captured-dovish)
proposed_indicator.rubric: 0 = BOJ board votes unified with market-consistent guidance and free from political appointments that contradict stated policy path; 1 = visible internal split (3+ dissenting votes or new appointee diverges materially from prevailing guidance); 2 = PM-appointed majority overrides market-implied tightening path, YCC-style suppression returns, or BOJ publicly subordinates rate policy to fiscal goals

boj-s-new-board-appointee-signals-dovish.rationale: The s2_japan story currently tracks USD/JPY, 30y JGB yield, and Takaichi plan financing clarity—but has no indicator for BOJ independence risk, which is the crucial transmission mechanism. Takaichi's appointment of a dovish board member (Sato) while simultaneously pushing a ¥370 trillion spending plan and urging BOJ to 'support growth' is textbook political pressure on central bank independence. If the BOJ is captured into suppressing yields to finance the Takaichi plan, the 30y JGB yield and USD/JPY indicators could move violently and non-linearly. A BOJ coherence indicator provides leading warning before those price indicators move.

jpy-hits-40-year-low-of-162-83-boj-hiked.headline: JPY hits 40-year low of ~162.83; BOJ hiked to 1% but yen slide continues as Takaichi fiscal plan pushes 10Y JGB yield toward 2.7–2.8%
jpy-hits-40-year-low-of-162-83-boj-hiked.summary: The Bank of Japan raised its policy rate to 1% on June 16 (highest since 1995) amid yen weakness and imported inflation from the Iran conflict, but USD/JPY still touched 162.83 on July 1—a four-decade low—as markets judged BOJ tightening insufficient versus the Fed. Japan spent a record ¥11.7 trillion (~$73.5bn) on FX intervention in April–May with limited durable effect; Takaichi's ¥370 trillion long-term investment blueprint is driving fiscal concerns and keeping JGB yields elevated near historic highs.

proposed_indicator.label: FX intervention firepower exhaustion (¥tn spent YTD vs. reserves)
proposed_indicator.rubric: 0 = <¥5tn spent, reserves comfortable; 1 = ¥5–15tn spent (elevated), intervention fatigue risk; 2 = >¥15tn spent or reserves visibly strained. Current: ~¥11.7tn spent YTD through May → score 1, trending toward 2 if yen remains near 162+.

jpy-hits-40-year-low-of-162-83-boj-hiked.rationale: s2 already tracks USD/JPY and 30Y JGB yield, but has no indicator capturing FX intervention reserve burn—a critical leading indicator for a disorderly yen move. Japan has spent a record ¥11.7tn in two months and the yen is still at 40-year lows; reserve exhaustion would remove the last non-BOJ backstop and could trigger a rapid carry-trade unwind like August 2024 but at a more extreme starting point. The Takaichi fiscal plan (¥370tn mobilization) also keeps the 'financing clarity' indicator at 1–2 given political pressure on BOJ to subordinate rate policy.

new-fed-chair-warsh-signals-data-depende.headline: New Fed Chair Warsh signals data-dependent 'new course' at Sintra: no forward guidance, inflation 'too high' at 4.2% CPI, rate-hike risk alive
new-fed-chair-warsh-signals-data-depende.summary: At the ECB's Sintra forum (June 30–July 1), new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh—who replaced Jerome Powell on May 22—confirmed CPI hit 4.2% YoY in May (3-year high driven by the Iran war oil shock), eliminated forward guidance, and left the door open to rate hikes, with nearly half of FOMC members projecting higher rates. Warsh noted inflation risks have 'come down' as energy prices ease post-MOU, but said price stability remains the overriding objective.

proposed_story.name: Fed regime shift under Warsh: opacity risk & rate-hike tail
proposed_story.why: The replacement of Powell with Warsh introduces a qualitatively new Fed communication regime: no forward guidance, alternative inflation metrics, task-force-driven policy redesign, and an explicit openness to rate hikes even as the administration pushes for cuts. With CPI at 4.2% (3-year high), a hawkish surprise at any upcoming FOMC meeting would simultaneously stress the basis trade (s1), blow out HY spreads (s3), hammer EM debtors (s4), accelerate yen carry unwind (s2), and detonate the AI capex financing cycle (s8). The opacity itself is a new systemic risk—markets cannot price the Fed reaction function.

indicators.label: Fed forward-guidance opacity index (0 = standard dot plot + forward guidance / 1 = reduced guidance / 2 = no guidance + alternative metrics)
indicators.rubric: 0 = traditional SEP/dot plot with explicit bias; 1 = reduced frequency or conditionality of guidance; 2 = guidance formally eliminated, reaction function opaque. Current: 2 (Warsh has explicitly removed forward guidance).

indicators.label: US CPI YoY (%)
indicators.rubric: Track monthly; above 3.5% = stress zone; above 4.0% = hike-risk zone. Current: 4.2% (May 2026).

indicators.label: FOMC rate-hike probability for next meeting (%, from OIS)
indicators.rubric: 0–10% = no near-term hike risk; 10–30% = elevated; >30% = active tightening threat. Track via CME FedWatch.

new-fed-chair-warsh-signals-data-depende.rationale: No existing story captures the systemic risk from a Fed leadership regime shift combined with opaque policy communication during a high-inflation, war-driven energy shock. This is not merely a 'higher rates' update to s8 or s1—it is a structural change to how the Fed signals, which affects the pricing of ALL risk assets and sovereign bonds simultaneously. The Warsh doctrine (no forward guidance, alternative data, task forces) means volatility-of-expectations is permanently higher regardless of where rates land.

bank-of-england-governor-bailey-flags-ri.headline: Bank of England Governor Bailey flags rising leverage in core government bonds, equity markets and private credit at Sintra as potential tail-risk-to-systemic-risk pipeline
bank-of-england-governor-bailey-flags-ri.summary: At the ECB Sintra forum (July 1), BoE Governor Andrew Bailey publicly flagged growing leverage in core government bond markets, hedge fund equity leverage, ETF leverage, and private credit—explicitly asking whether these tail risks could 'move into a broader consequence.' This comes as the BIS Annual Economic Report (late June) independently flagged NBFI leverage and rising public debt as two of three key global financial stability vulnerabilities.
bank-of-england-governor-bailey-flags-ri.rationale: Bailey's explicit public warning about leverage in core government bond markets maps directly onto s1 (Treasury basis trade / hidden leverage) and s3 (private credit). The fact that both the BoE Governor and the BIS—within the same week—are naming the same NBFI/leverage cluster as a systemic tail risk elevates the signal-to-noise ratio significantly. The MOVE index is the existing indicator best placed to reflect stress materializing in this channel. This also reinforces s3's 'large-fund redemption gates' indicator given Bailey's specific call-out of private credit as a candidate for tail-to-systemic contagion.

us-china-tariff-regime-restructured-post.headline: US-China tariff regime restructured post-Supreme Court IEEPA ruling: effective rate ~30% under Section 301/232 framework; USTR Section 301 forced-labor investigation adds potential 12.5% layer
us-china-tariff-regime-restructured-post.summary: Following the US Supreme Court's strike-down of IEEPA-based tariffs (effective February 2026), US tariffs on China were restructured around a 15% global tariff plus remaining Section 301/232 duties, keeping the effective rate near 30%—still the highest of any country. On June 2, USTR proposed an additional 12.5% tariff on China stemming from a new Section 301 forced-labor investigation, with the two investigations set to conclude this summer, threatening to push effective rates higher again.
us-china-tariff-regime-restructured-post.rationale: The USTR's June 2 proposed 12.5% Section 301 forced-labor tariff on China, with a summer conclusion date, is a material new escalation event that fits directly under s5's 'New tariff/anti-dumping actions' indicator (moving it from 1-rising to 2-escalating). The IEEPA restructuring also changes the legal architecture of US-China trade policy in ways that affect the durability and predictability of the current ~30% effective tariff floor, creating ongoing uncertainty that compounds China's domestic deflation dynamic (CPI near zero, PPI negative, fourth year of deflation per CNBC March 2026).

japan-s-takaichi-government-pauses-boj-b.headline: Japan's Takaichi government pauses BOJ bond-taper programme under political pressure; 10Y JGB yield near 30-year high of ~2.7-2.8%, Oxford Economics raises end-2026 forecast to 2.8%
japan-s-takaichi-government-pauses-boj-b.summary: A government economic panel member confirmed on July 2 that Tokyo's pressure on the BOJ about bond-market instability led the BOJ to pause its JGB taper programme from next fiscal year. Meanwhile, the 10Y JGB yield has surged from ~2.1% in March to ~2.7% now (near October 1996 highs), with Oxford Economics raising its end-2026 forecast to 2.8%, driven by Takaichi's ¥370tn spending blueprint and concerns over fiscal financing—representing a direct stress on the 's2_japan' 30-year JGB yield indicator.
japan-s-takaichi-government-pauses-boj-b.target_indicator: Takaichi plan financing clarity (0 ok / 1 unclear / 2 disorderly)
japan-s-takaichi-government-pauses-boj-b.rationale: The July 2 Reuters-sourced report that the Takaichi government directly pressured the BOJ into pausing its QT programme—confirmed by a government panel member—is a material new development that pushes the 'Takaichi plan financing clarity' indicator from 1 (unclear) to 2 (disorderly), because political interference in BOJ balance-sheet policy to suppress the fiscal cost of spending plans is precisely the 'fiscal dominance' scenario this indicator is designed to detect. The 60bp surge in 10Y JGB yields since March (to near 30-year highs) and Oxford Economics' upward revision to 2.8% for end-2026 confirm that markets are pricing this deterioration. This also updates the '30-year JGB yield' indicator materially upward.

us-fiscal-dominance-accelerating-obbba-d.headline: US fiscal dominance accelerating: OBBBA debt trajectory, $1.05T annual interest bill, 4.2% CPI, and new Fed Chair Warsh abandons forward guidance
us-fiscal-dominance-accelerating-obbba-d.summary: As of July 2026, annualized US interest payments on the national debt have exceeded $1.05 trillion — surpassing the defense budget — following the OBBBA (signed July 4, 2025) which CBO scores as adding $3.4T to debt over 10 years. New Fed Chair Kevin Warsh dropped forward guidance in June, and May 2026 CPI printed 4.2% YoY (highest since April 2023), creating a fiscal-vs-monetary collision dynamic that is bear-steepening the Treasury curve with the 10-year hovering near 4.4%.

proposed_story.name: US Fiscal Dominance & Treasury Term Premium Blowout
proposed_story.why: The combination of OBBBA-driven structural deficit expansion (~$3.4-5T added debt over 10 years), interest costs eclipsing the defense budget at $1.05T/yr, a new Fed chair abandoning forward guidance amid 4.2% CPI, and a bear-steepening curve creates a qualitatively new risk: fiscal dominance, where the Treasury's borrowing needs constrain monetary policy and compress the Fed's degrees of freedom. This is distinct from s1_basis_trade (which focuses on hidden leverage in the repo/basis market) and s4_dev_debt (which focuses on EM). It is a US sovereign fiscal-financial feedback loop risk: sticky inflation + structural deficit + term premium rise = potential investor strike on long-duration Treasuries, which would cascade into every rate-sensitive sector. The Bruegel analysis explicitly models a path to debt crisis if future primary surpluses of >3% GDP are required.

indicators.label: US 10-year Treasury yield (%)
indicators.rubric: Tracks the term premium signal; above 4.5% = elevated, above 5% = acute fiscal stress signal

indicators.label: US annual net interest outlay vs. defense budget ratio (x)
indicators.rubric: Interest/defense ratio >1.0x means debt service is crowding out core spending; tracks fiscal dominance severity

indicators.label: Fed forward guidance status (0 intact / 1 data-only / 2 abandoned/contradicted)
indicators.rubric: Measures policy credibility and rate-path uncertainty; Warsh's June 2026 removal of forward guidance = 1; explicit contradiction of committee stance = 2

indicators.label: CBO 10-year deficit projection under current law ($T)
indicators.rubric: Baseline structural trajectory; $3-4T = elevated, >$5T including interest = acute

us-fiscal-dominance-accelerating-obbba-d.rationale: No existing story captures US sovereign fiscal dominance as a self-contained risk. s1_basis_trade covers repo/leverage; s4_dev_debt covers EM. The OBBBA + Warsh + 4.2% CPI + $1.05T interest bill is a distinct systemic node: if the Treasury curve bear-steepens past 5% on the 10-year while the Fed is constrained by inflation, it creates a doom loop for rate-sensitive sectors (CRE, regional banks, private credit, pensions, AI capex) simultaneously. The Bruegel model explicitly warns of debt unsustainability if required primary surpluses exceed 3% of GDP.

eu-eliminates-de-minimis-customs-exempti.headline: EU eliminates de minimis customs exemption July 1, 2026 — redirecting 'China Shock 2.0' trade flows and triggering new anti-dumping pressure
eu-eliminates-de-minimis-customs-exempti.summary: The EU's Council formally eliminated the de minimis customs exemption for low-value parcels effective July 1, 2026, directly targeting the surge of Chinese e-commerce exports that grew 41.8% YoY to the EU in 2025 as China redirected trade away from US markets. This closes a key circumvention channel and is expected to accelerate EU anti-dumping actions, raise Chinese export deflation pressure back domestically, and shift the geography of the 'China Shock 2.0' dynamic into Southeast Asia and Africa.

proposed_indicator.label: EU/third-market trade barrier closures against Chinese exports (0 open / 1 new barriers / 2 coordinated block)
proposed_indicator.rubric: Tracks the degree to which non-US markets are absorbing or deflecting China's export surplus; as third-market channels close, deflation pressure rebounds domestically and anti-dumping actions escalate globally. 0 = channels open; 1 = one major jurisdiction closes a channel (e.g., EU de minimis, July 2026); 2 = coordinated G7/multilateral action

eu-eliminates-de-minimis-customs-exempti.rationale: s5_china_deflation already tracks PPI, USD/CNY, and tariff/anti-dumping actions — but none of the indicators captures the third-market channel dynamics that are now the primary valve for China's export overhang. As the EU closes its de minimis loophole (July 1, 2026, just enacted), Chinese exporters face tighter exit routes, which will either intensify domestic deflation or drive more aggressive dumping into other markets. This is a story_gap: the mechanism is within s5 but the indicator doesn't exist.

boj-policy-rate-to-fiscal-plan-collision.headline: BOJ policy-rate-to-fiscal-plan collision: Takaichi's ¥370tn mobilization plan pressures BOJ to stay accommodative while inflation demands hikes — carry unwind risk building
boj-policy-rate-to-fiscal-plan-collision.summary: PM Takaichi's long-term economic strategy targeting ¥370 trillion in public-private investment through FY2040 has explicitly called for BOJ monetary policy to 'support growth objectives,' while simultaneously the BOJ hiked to 1.0% in late June and board member Tamura targets 2%. New BOJ board appointee Sato signaled more accommodative views, indicating a deepening internal split. With the yen at a four-decade low and JGB yields near 30-year highs, the carry trade unwind risk is acute heading into the July 30-31 BOJ meeting.

proposed_indicator.label: BOJ board policy cohesion / political interference risk (0 unified-independent / 1 split-board / 2 fiscal-dominance-capture)
proposed_indicator.rubric: Tracks the degree to which BOJ policy independence is intact. 0 = unanimous or near-unanimous decisions with no political pressure; 1 = split vote (as in April 2026 6-3) and/or government publicly demanding accommodative policy; 2 = BOJ explicitly subordinates rate path to fiscal objectives or new politically-aligned board majority. Current state: 1 (split board, Takaichi plan calls for BOJ support, new accommodative appointee Sato)

boj-policy-rate-to-fiscal-plan-collision.rationale: s2_japan tracks the Takaichi plan financing clarity indicator but as a binary rubric. The more precise and actionable risk is the BOJ's institutional independence being eroded by fiscal pressure — analogous to the Bank of England pre-2022 mini-budget episode. A split board (6-3 in April), a new accommodative appointee, and government language demanding policy support are all present simultaneously. If the BOJ blinks at the July 30-31 meeting under political pressure, it could trigger a rapid yen overshooting and a global carry unwind. This is a story_gap: the mechanism is within s2 but the indicator doesn't exist.

fed-chair-warsh-removes-forward-guidance.headline: Fed Chair Warsh removes forward guidance at ECB Sintra Forum (July 1); US CPI at 4.2% YoY — largest since April 2023 — creates 'higher for longer' risk with no rate-cut signals
fed-chair-warsh-removes-forward-guidance.summary: At the ECB Forum in Sintra on July 1, new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh removed traditional forward guidance in favour of pure data dependence and stated 'prices are too high', while US CPI for May 2026 printed at 4.2% YoY. The Fed funds rate sits at 3.50–3.75% with no imminent cut signals, while markets had priced easing.

proposed_indicator.label: Fed reaction-function opacity (0 transparent/1 uncertain/2 opaque)
proposed_indicator.rubric: 0 = Fed chair provides clear forward guidance and rate path; 1 = forward guidance removed, pure data dependence, market repricing underway; 2 = active contradiction between Fed communications and market pricing, or abrupt pivot creating financial conditions shock

fed-chair-warsh-removes-forward-guidance.rationale: The combination of a 4.2% CPI print (highest in 3 years), a new Fed chair who has eliminated forward guidance, and a Fed funds rate at 3.50–3.75% with no clear easing path directly worsens the US 10y real yield and broad dollar index indicators in s4_dev_debt, while also compressing the rate-cut buffer for s9_cre and s11_consumer_debt. No existing indicator in any story captures the Fed regime/communication shift itself — this proposed indicator would flag when the reaction function becomes a source of volatility rather than an anchor.

bis-annual-report-and-boe-governor-baile.headline: BIS Annual Report and BOE Governor Bailey at Sintra flag acute leverage build-up in government bond basis trades, equity hedge funds, and private credit as tail-risk-to-systemic risk inflection point
bis-annual-report-and-boe-governor-baile.summary: The BIS June 2026 Annual Report identified rising NBFI leverage and non-bank vulnerabilities as a top structural risk. On July 1 at ECB Sintra, Bank of England Governor Bailey explicitly flagged 'increase in leverage in core government bond markets', rising hedge fund equity leverage, and ETF leverage, asking whether private credit 'can move from tail risk into a broader consequence.'

proposed_indicator.label: Regulatory/central-bank NBFI leverage alert level (0 none/1 flagged/2 action)
proposed_indicator.rubric: 0 = No major central bank or BIS warning on NBFI leverage; 1 = BIS Annual Report or G10 central bank governor publicly flags NBFI leverage as tail risk; 2 = Formal macroprudential action, margin call cascade, or emergency liquidity facility triggered by NBFI leverage

bis-annual-report-and-boe-governor-baile.rationale: s1_basis_trade and s3_private_credit each capture pieces of the NBFI leverage ecosystem, but neither has an indicator that tracks the meta-signal of regulatory/central bank alarm level. Bailey's Sintra comments on July 1 are the most senior real-time warning since the LDI crisis — explicitly linking Treasury basis leverage, equity hedge fund leverage, and private credit in a single systemic concern. The BIS Annual Report (end-June) corroborates. A cross-story indicator tracking this alarm level would serve as an early warning for contagion across s1, s3, and s21.

japan-30-year-jgb-yield-hits-4-04-july-2.headline: Japan 30-year JGB yield hits 4.04% (July 2) and yen slides to 40-year low; record ¥11.73tn MoF intervention failed to hold
japan-30-year-jgb-yield-hits-4-04-july-2.summary: Japan's 30-year JGB yield reached 4.04% on July 2, 2026, up ~108bps year-on-year, while the 10-year topped 2.75%–2.8% (a 29-year high). The yen has weakened to its lowest level since 1986, and Japan's MoF deployed a record ¥11.73 trillion ($73.35bn) FX intervention in April–May that failed to sustain a rally; Bank of America lowered its year-end USD/JPY forecast to 152 from 157 but the pair is threatening 160. The Takaichi government's ¥370 trillion investment strategy is stoking fiscal concern, with sustained bond selling pressure and a weak 10-year auction this week reinforcing the disorderly backdrop.

proposed_indicator.label: Takaichi fiscal plan JGB supply impulse (¥tn additional issuance vs. prior medium-term fiscal plan)
proposed_indicator.rubric: Measure the incremental JGB gross issuance implied by the Takaichi ¥370tn investment plan vs. the prior Cabinet Office medium-term fiscal framework. Values: <¥10tn incremental = 0 (ok); ¥10–30tn = 1 (unclear/pressured); >¥30tn or no credible offset = 2 (disorderly). Supplement with 30-year JGB yield level: <3.0% = 0; 3.0–3.5% = 1; >3.5% = 2. Current: 4.04% → score 2.

japan-30-year-jgb-yield-hits-4-04-july-2.rationale: The existing s2 indicator 'Takaichi plan financing clarity (0 ok / 1 unclear / 2 disorderly)' is already present but is qualitative. The 30-year JGB yield at 4.04% and record failed MoF FX intervention signal that the market has already rendered a verdict of '2 disorderly' on fiscal financing, yet the tracker lacks a quantitative anchor to that judgment. A ¥-denominated JGB supply impulse metric linked to the ¥370tn plan would make the indicator more actionable and harder to game with diplomatic language. The 30-year yield is the key transmission mechanism to global carry trade unwind risk.

china-s-new-outbound-investment-regulati.headline: China's new outbound investment regulations (effective July 1, 2026) impose sweeping controls on cross-border transfers of sensitive tech, data, and goods — a new structural chokepoint
china-s-new-outbound-investment-regulati.summary: On July 1, 2026, China's new outbound investment regulations took effect, requiring prior authorization for cross-border transfers of restricted goods, technology, services, and data by Chinese-owned or China-domiciled entities. This creates a new compliance and supply-chain severance mechanism that goes beyond export controls to cover investment flows and data transfers, institutionalizing Beijing's economic-coercion toolkit in ways that no existing tracker indicator fully captures.

proposed_story.name: China outbound investment & data-transfer controls as economic coercion
proposed_story.why: China's July 1, 2026 outbound investment regulations create a new category of cross-border restriction that is distinct from export controls (which govern outbound goods) or import tariffs. By requiring prior authorization for overseas investment and cross-border data/technology transfers by any entity within China's jurisdiction, Beijing has effectively weaponized capital outflows and IP transfers as a coercion tool. This threatens JV structures, technology licensing, cloud-data flows, and supply-chain financing for multinationals with significant China footprints. It also raises the risk of forced technology transfers in reverse (retain access = share IP). No existing story in the tracker captures this 'investment coercion' vector — s6 covers minerals, s19 covers lithography export controls, but neither covers the investment/data-transfer dimension affecting all sectors simultaneously.

indicators.label: China outbound investment restriction scope (0 narrow/sectoral / 1 broad/multi-sector / 2 sweeping/all-sector with retroactive review)
indicators.rubric: Score 0 if restrictions cover only defense-adjacent sectors with clear carve-outs for civilian commerce; score 1 if restrictions cover technology, data, and critical materials across multiple civilian sectors requiring prior authorization; score 2 if restrictions are applied retroactively, include broad data-transfer obligations, and are actively enforced against third-country entities. Current: 1 (multi-sector, data + tech + goods, effective July 1 2026).

indicators.label: Multinational JV restructuring / China-exit announcements (count per quarter, rolling)
indicators.rubric: Track announced exits, forced restructurings, or JV wind-downs citing China regulatory risk per quarter. 0–5 = 0 (calm); 6–15 = 1 (rising); >15 or any Fortune 100 forced exit = 2 (acute). Proxy for actual economic transmission of investment coercion.

indicators.label: Cross-border data-transfer enforcement actions by MOFCOM/CAC (0 none / 1 warning-stage / 2 active penalties)
indicators.rubric: Score 0 if no enforcement actions against non-Chinese entities for cross-border data transfer violations; score 1 if formal investigations or warning notices issued; score 2 if financial penalties or operational suspensions imposed on multinationals for data-transfer non-compliance. Current: 0 (rules just took effect July 1).

china-s-new-outbound-investment-regulati.rationale: The July 1, 2026 outbound investment regulations represent a qualitatively new economic coercion tool — distinct from export controls — that affects the full universe of multinationals with China operations, not just critical-mineral or semiconductor supply chains. The existing tracker has no story that captures investment-coercion risk (as opposed to goods-export coercion). Given escalating US–China tensions (Pentagon blacklists, MOFCOM counter-blacklists, rare-earth controls), this mechanism is likely to be progressively weaponized and deserves a dedicated story. Proposed level 6 reflects significant structural risk that is currently latent but could transmit rapidly via corporate earnings shocks, FDI retrenchment, and supply-chain disruption.

japan-basic-policy-shock-jgb-10yr-approa.headline: Japan 'Basic Policy Shock': JGB 10yr Approaches 2.8% as Government Blueprint Pressures BOJ Dovishness
japan-basic-policy-shock-jgb-10yr-approa.summary: Japan's June 30 government economic policy blueprint signalled that 'appropriate monetary policy conduct is extremely important,' widely read as an attempt to constrain further BOJ rate hikes; combined with a weak 10-year JGB auction and a ¥370 trillion public-private investment plan, the 10-year JGB yield climbed toward 2.8%—its highest since October 1996. Markets now face a dual threat: a weakened yen near 40-year lows and rising JGB yields that undermine the carry-trade unwind thesis.

proposed_indicator.label: BOJ_independence_risk
proposed_indicator.rubric: 0 = BOJ operating freely with no political interference; 1 = government fiscal-policy documents or statements implicitly constrain BOJ path; 2 = explicit political pressure causing BOJ to pause hikes despite inflation above target or yen at crisis lows

japan-basic-policy-shock-jgb-10yr-approa.rationale: The 'Basic Policy shock' is a qualitatively new development that the existing s2_japan indicators (USD/JPY level, 30yr JGB yield, Takaichi plan financing clarity) do not fully capture: it specifically measures whether the BOJ retains independence to hike when inflation is above target and the yen is at 40-year lows. A score of 1 is now appropriate. The 10yr JGB at 2.8% and weak auction also push the existing 30yr yield indicator higher. This is a medium-term carry-unwind trigger and sovereign-financing risk.

us-cpi-at-4-2-yoy-may-2026-new-fed-chair.headline: US CPI at 4.2% YoY (May 2026); New Fed Chair Warsh Abandons Forward Guidance
us-cpi-at-4-2-yoy-may-2026-new-fed-chair.summary: US headline CPI rose 4.2% YoY through May 2026—the largest 12-month increase since April 2023—driven by energy and tariff pass-through. Following its June meeting, new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh removed traditional forward guidance in favour of pure data dependence, while keeping rates at 3.50–3.75%, raising risks that markets cannot price the policy path and that a stagflation dynamic could entrench.

proposed_story.name: US stagflation & Fed credibility cliff
proposed_story.why: CPI at 4.2% YoY with a new Fed Chair who has eliminated forward guidance creates a qualitatively different regime: markets cannot anchor rate expectations, real wages are being eroded, and tariff-plus-energy cost-push inflation is sticky even as growth slows. This is distinct from existing stories (s11_consumer_debt captures downstream consumer stress; s1_basis_trade captures Treasury volatility) because the primary risk is a regime shift in US monetary policy credibility itself—if Warsh pivots to hikes to defend the dollar or price stability, the entire leveraged-finance stack (basis trade, private credit, CRE) re-prices simultaneously.

indicators.label: US_CPI_YoY_pct
indicators.rubric: Current 12-month US headline CPI (%). Risk rises nonlinearly above 3.5%; threshold of 4.5% = acute.

indicators.label: Fed_forward_guidance_clarity
indicators.rubric: 0 = explicit forward guidance in place; 1 = data-dependent with no path signal (current); 2 = contradictory signals or emergency pivot language

indicators.label: US_real_wage_growth_YoY_pct
indicators.rubric: Average hourly earnings minus CPI. Negative = stagflationary squeeze on consumer and political pressure for Fed ease despite inflation.

indicators.label: US_tariff_Section122_legal_status
indicators.rubric: 0 = upheld / stable; 1 = legal challenge under appeal (current—court ruled exceeded authority May 7); 2 = struck down, forcing policy vacuum

us-cpi-at-4-2-yoy-may-2026-new-fed-chair.rationale: No existing story covers the interaction of a 4.2% CPI print, a new Fed Chair who has removed forward guidance, and a tariff legal challenge—these together constitute a distinct US monetary-credibility risk cluster. The nearest story (s1_basis_trade) focuses on structural plumbing; this story is about the macro regime anchor. A level-7 severity reflects that this is already partially manifest (CPI is live, guidance is gone) but a full stagflation entrenchment has not yet occurred.

japan-stagflation-risk-materialises-boj-.headline: Japan Stagflation Risk Materialises: BOJ Holds at 0.75%, Core Inflation Forecast Raised to 2.8%, Growth Cut to 0.5%
japan-stagflation-risk-materialises-boj-.summary: At its April 28 meeting (6-3 vote), the BOJ kept rates at 0.75% while sharply raising its core CPI forecast to 2.8% (from 1.9%) and cutting FY2026 growth to 0.5% (from 1.0%), citing Iran-war energy cost pass-through and deteriorating terms of trade. Oxford Economics described a 'very light stagflation-like situation' for Japan, with real disposable incomes persistently negative—a dynamic that complicates the BOJ's ability to hike to defend the yen while the government's Basic Policy blueprint simultaneously pushes for accommodative monetary conditions.
japan-stagflation-risk-materialises-boj-.rationale: The existing s2_japan indicator 'Takaichi plan financing clarity (0 ok / 1 unclear / 2 disorderly)' should be updated toward 2 (disorderly): the government's ¥370 trillion spending blueprint combined with explicit pressure on the BOJ to stay accommodative, a 6-3 split vote, and a core inflation forecast well above the 2% target creates financing-clarity disorder. The yen near 40-year lows amplifies the USD/JPY risk indicator. Taken together with item #1 above (the Basic Policy shock), s2_japan is the highest-priority story requiring indicator updates this week.

japan-basic-policy-shock-jgb-yields-near.headline: Japan 'Basic Policy Shock': JGB yields near 30-year high, yen at 40-year low as government blueprint seen curbing BOJ hikes
japan-basic-policy-shock-jgb-yields-near.summary: Japan's June 30 economic policy blueprint was read by markets as an attempt to rein in BOJ rate hikes, sending 10-year JGB yields toward 2.8% (highest since 1996) and USD/JPY grinding near 162 — 40-year yen lows — in what MUFG called the 'Basic Policy shock'. Super-long JGB yields face further upside pressure while uncertainty over ¥370 trillion spending plan financing lingers.
japan-basic-policy-shock-jgb-yields-near.target_indicator: 30-year JGB yield (%) + USD/JPY + Takaichi plan financing clarity
japan-basic-policy-shock-jgb-yields-near.rationale: Directly updates three existing s2_japan indicators: 10y JGB yield surging toward multi-decade high signals fiscal/monetary credibility stress; USD/JPY near 40-year lows flags carry-unwind tail risk; government blueprint language (seen as reining in BOJ) moves 'Takaichi plan financing clarity' toward 2 (disorderly). The confluence of all three indicators simultaneously worsening is high materiality for global rate and carry markets.

hormuz-re-escalation-july-7-three-tanker.headline: Hormuz re-escalation July 7: Three tankers struck, US launches new strikes on Iran, oil jumps 6%, Iranian oil license revoked
hormuz-re-escalation-july-7-three-tanker.summary: On July 7 three merchant vessels were struck by projectiles in the Strait of Hormuz — the highest single-day attack count since late April. The US immediately revoked Iran's oil-sales license, launched fresh strikes on IRGC sites near the strait, and Trump declared the ceasefire 'over', pushing oil prices up more than 6%.
hormuz-re-escalation-july-7-three-tanker.target_indicator: Hormuz/Red Sea status (0 open / 1 contested / 2 closed) + WTI crude oil (USD/bbl)
hormuz-re-escalation-july-7-three-tanker.rationale: The July 7 attacks represent a direct, material deterioration of the Hormuz/Red Sea status indicator (moves from 1 contested back toward 2 closed) and a sharp upward jolt to WTI. The US license revocation and resumed CENTCOM strikes signal the June 17 Islamabad MOU is now functionally suspended, raising the probability of a sustained supply disruption — the IEA had already characterised the earlier closure as 'the largest supply disruption in history of the global oil market'. Directly actionable for s20_sea_lanes indicator values.

genius-act-july-18-rulemaking-deadline-i.headline: GENIUS Act July 18 rulemaking deadline imminent; stablecoin supply hits record $322B; MiCA ends USDT's EU access
genius-act-july-18-rulemaking-deadline-i.summary: Six US federal agencies face a July 18 statutory deadline to finalise GENIUS Act rules; final rules still outstanding as of July 8, creating a 10-day implementation friction window. Simultaneously, MiCA's hard enforcement deadline expired July 1 with USDT delisted from Coinbase, Kraken, Binance EU and others — forming a two-tier regulated vs. offshore stablecoin market. Total USD stablecoin supply hit a record $322B in June.
genius-act-july-18-rulemaking-deadline-i.target_indicator: GENIUS implementation friction (0 smooth / 1 gaps / 2 problems) + Total USD stablecoin supply ($bn)
genius-act-july-18-rulemaking-deadline-i.rationale: The July 18 deadline for final GENIUS rules with agencies still in proposed-rule stage moves the implementation friction indicator to 1 (gaps). Record $322B supply is material for the supply indicator. The MiCA USDT delistings create a liquidity fragmentation risk — a large stablecoin losing regulated-venue access in the world's second-largest economy is a direct peg-stability and redemption-pressure stress scenario relevant to the 'largest stablecoin max peg deviation' indicator. Two-tier market formation is a medium-materiality story-gap for the tracker.

sec-treasury-clearing-mandate-cross-marg.headline: SEC Treasury-clearing mandate cross-margining risk: Dec 2026 cash-clearing deadline on track but collateral crunch for basis traders identified
sec-treasury-clearing-mandate-cross-marg.summary: Chicago Fed Letters 516 & 517 (2026) confirm the SEC's Dec 31 2026 cash-Treasury clearing and mid-2027 repo clearing deadlines are on track with CME's CMESC receiving SEC approval Dec 2025 and targeting Q2 2026 launch. However, Fed analysis finds the mandate will sharply increase initial margin requirements for leveraged basis-trade funds unless cross-margining programs are expanded — a structural liquidity squeeze risk not yet in the tracker.

proposed_indicator.label: CCP cross-margining adoption for basis trades (0 expanded / 1 partial / 2 not adopted)
proposed_indicator.rubric: Score 0 if FICC, CME and ICE have all expanded cross-margining programs covering Treasury cash + futures + repo positions before Dec 2026 clearing go-live; score 1 if one or two CCPs have partial programs with significant basis-trader IM increase expected; score 2 if no material cross-margining expansion occurs, implying a collateral crunch that forces basis-trade deleveraging at clearing go-live.

sec-treasury-clearing-mandate-cross-marg.rationale: The existing s1_basis_trade indicators (SOFR-EFFR, MOVE, SEC central clearing on-track flag) do not capture the specific collateral-squeeze mechanism identified by the Chicago Fed: without cross-margining expansion, the Dec 2026 clearing mandate forces basis-trade funds to post sharply higher IM at CCPs simultaneously — a structural forced-deleveraging event that could amplify Treasury market stress at the exact moment of go-live. This is a story gap because the mechanism is now well-documented in official Fed research and the 6-month countdown window makes it time-sensitive for a risk tracker.

imf-july-8-weo-update-global-growth-down.headline: IMF July 8 WEO Update: Global growth downgraded to 2.5% in 2026 as Middle East energy shock drives inflation and EM debt stress
imf-july-8-weo-update-global-growth-down.summary: The IMF's July 8, 2026 World Economic Outlook update cited the Middle East conflict as driving sharp energy price increases, renewed inflation, and tighter monetary policy expectations globally, cutting its 2026 global growth forecast to 2.5% — the weakest EMDE per-capita growth since COVID. Rising debt is pushing up EMDE borrowing costs, and the IMF flags 'financial stress' and 'commodity market disruption' as key downside risks to an already fragile outlook.

proposed_indicator.label: IMF/World Bank growth forecast for EMDEs (% YoY)
proposed_indicator.rubric: Track the IMF WEO and World Bank GEP headline EMDE growth forecast as a leading indicator of sovereign stress; a cut below 3.5% signals rising fiscal pressure and missed-coupon risk for heavily indebted EMDEs. Current: 2.5% (July 2026 WEO). Pair with 'New IMF programs / missed coupons' ordinal.

imf-july-8-weo-update-global-growth-down.rationale: The s4_dev_debt story tracks the developing-world debt crisis but has no indicator for the multilateral growth-forecast signal. The IMF July 8 cut to 2.5% global growth (with EMDEs the weakest since COVID) is directly causally linked to deteriorating EM sovereign debt service capacity; it is distinct from the existing TIPS real yield and dollar index indicators and fills a genuine gap in forward-looking stress detection.

scotus-voids-ieepa-tariffs-new-15-global.headline: SCOTUS voids IEEPA tariffs; new 15% global baseline + 12.5% Section 301 tariff proposed; USTR comment window closes today (July 10)
scotus-voids-ieepa-tariffs-new-15-global.summary: After the Supreme Court struck down IEEPA tariff authority (Feb 20, 2026), the administration replaced it with a 15% global Section 122 tariff (expiring July 24) and simultaneously proposed a 12.5% Section 301 forced-labor tariff on 60 countries; the Section 301 comment period closes today. A separate 25% Section 301 tariff has been proposed on Brazil, and USTR is finalising up to $30bn in China tariff relief from the May 2026 Trump-Xi summit framework.

proposed_story.name: US tariff architecture in flux: IEEPA void, Section 301/122 transition, and trade-deal conditionality
proposed_story.why: The SCOTUS IEEPA decision in February 2026 destroyed the legal foundation of Trump's tariff regime, forcing a rushed replacement via Section 122 (expiring July 24) and Section 301 investigations. The resulting legal uncertainty — with overlapping and potentially invalid tariff stacks, active litigation, appeals, and a simultaneous China détente offering selective relief — creates a 'tariff fog' that disrupts corporate supply-chain planning, affects inflation (US CPI already 4.2%), and introduces binary event risk (court injunctions, WTO challenges, deal collapse) distinct from the China-specific deflation/PPI story in s5_china_deflation.

indicators.label: Effective US average tariff rate on all imports (%)
indicators.rubric: Track the Tax Foundation / USITC blended effective tariff rate; an increase above 10% signals meaningful cost-push inflation and supply-chain repricing. Currently ~3–4% blended, up sharply from <2% pre-2025.

indicators.label: Tariff legal/judicial stability (0 stable / 1 one regime invalidated / 2 cascading invalidations)
indicators.rubric: 0 = existing tariff regime is legally intact; 1 = one major tariff mechanism struck down or enjoined (current: IEEPA voided, Section 122 challenged); 2 = multiple regimes simultaneously enjoined creating enforcement vacuum.

indicators.label: US-China trade truce durability (0 holding / 1 conditional / 2 collapsing)
indicators.rubric: 0 = Busan framework fully operative with tariff reductions proceeding; 1 = deal intact but conditioned on Iran/military-linked secondary tariff threats or unresolved Section 301 escalation; 2 = framework collapsed with retaliatory tariff snapback.

indicators.label: Section 122 → Section 301 transition cliff (days to expiry / replacement clarity)
indicators.rubric: Section 122 expires July 24; Section 301 replacement requires final rule. Track days to expiry against rule finalisation status. A gap (expiry without replacement) = effective tariff holiday creating trade-flow distortions and fiscal shortfall.

scotus-voids-ieepa-tariffs-new-15-global.rationale: No existing story captures the structural legal fragility of the US tariff regime itself. s5_china_deflation tracks Chinese PPI and yuan dynamics; s4_dev_debt tracks EM sovereign stress; but neither captures the US-specific tariff architecture risk: IEEPA voided, Section 122 expiring in 14 days, Section 301 replacement with uncertain legal standing, active court appeals, and a China détente with binary collapse risk. This is a distinct, high-materiality macro risk with direct inflation, fiscal, and financial-market channels — and the comment period closing today marks a key decision node.

new-fed-chair-warsh-drops-forward-guidan.headline: New Fed Chair Warsh drops forward guidance, holds at 3.5–3.75%; US CPI at 4.2% YoY — highest since April 2023 — amid oil shock
new-fed-chair-warsh-drops-forward-guidan.summary: Following Kevin Warsh's first FOMC meeting as Fed Chair (June 2026), the Fed removed traditional forward guidance and adopted pure data dependence, holding the federal funds rate at 3.50–3.75%. May 2026 US CPI printed 4.2% YoY — a multi-year high driven by the Hormuz energy shock — leaving the Fed effectively trapped between a slowing economy and re-accelerating inflation, with no forward signal to anchor markets.

proposed_story.name: Fed policy credibility trap: stagflation echo, leadership change, and no-guidance regime
proposed_story.why: The combination of (a) a new Fed Chair removing forward guidance, (b) CPI re-accelerating to 4.2% on an energy shock, and (c) the Fed on hold with rates at 3.5–3.75% creates a unique policy-credibility risk not captured in any existing story. Without forward guidance, bond markets lose their anchor, term premium can spike unpredictably, and the basis trade (s1) and CRE maturity wall (s9) are both sensitive to a disorderly repricing of the front end. This is structurally different from normal rate-path uncertainty because it involves a deliberate communications rupture by new leadership during an active inflation shock.

indicators.label: US CPI YoY (%)
indicators.rubric: Track monthly CPI YoY; above 4% indicates the Fed is behind the curve relative to its 2% target. Currently 4.2% (May 2026). An increase above 5% would signal acute credibility risk.

indicators.label: Fed forward guidance regime (0 standard / 1 modified / 2 removed)
indicators.rubric: 0 = traditional dot-plot + press-conference forward guidance; 1 = guidance modified or de-emphasised; 2 = guidance formally removed in favour of pure data dependence (current: 2, post-Warsh June 2026 meeting).

indicators.label: US 5y5y inflation breakeven (%)
indicators.rubric: Forward inflation expectations; a move above 2.75% signals market loss of confidence in the Fed's long-run inflation anchor, which would force a hawkish response even against a slowing economy.

indicators.label: Fed credibility stress (0 anchored / 1 drifting / 2 unanchored)
indicators.rubric: Composite of: inflation vs. target gap, breakeven de-anchoring, and market-implied rate-path volatility. 0 = CPI <3% and breakevens stable; 1 = CPI 3–5% with breakevens drifting; 2 = CPI >5% or breakevens above 3%.

new-fed-chair-warsh-drops-forward-guidan.rationale: No existing story in the tracker covers US Federal Reserve institutional credibility risk. The removal of forward guidance by a new Fed Chair during an active energy-driven inflation shock is a distinct systemic risk: it increases volatility across all fixed-income-sensitive stories (s1_basis_trade, s2_japan carry unwind, s9_cre maturity wall, s14_pensions LDI risk) and removes the policy anchor that other risk stories implicitly rely on. The June 2026 regime change is recent, material, and has no current tracker home.

new-risk-dm-central-bank-independence-un.headline: New Risk: DM Central Bank Independence Under Simultaneous Political Pressure — Japan 'Basic Policy Shock' + US Fed Chair Warsh Removes Forward Guidance
new-risk-dm-central-bank-independence-un.summary: Within the same week (July 7-13), two G7 central banks face explicit political interference: Japan's government briefly drafted language that would have discouraged BoJ rate hikes (the 'Basic Policy shock'), while in the US, new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh eliminated all forward guidance after the June FOMC meeting, operating under an administration that publicly demanded rate-cut 'commitments' from Fed chair candidates. Simultaneous erosion of credible monetary anchor across the two largest bond markets amplifies macro volatility risk in a high-debt, high-inflation environment.

proposed_story.name: DM Central Bank Independence Erosion
proposed_story.why: Simultaneous political pressure on the BoJ (Japan 'Basic Policy shock' — government drafting language to discourage rate hikes) and the US Fed (Trump administration demanding rate-cut commitments; new Chair Warsh scrapping forward guidance) represents a structural risk not captured by any existing story. If credible monetary anchors in both the US and Japan are perceived as politically compromised, long-end sovereign yields in both markets could reprice sharply higher (compounding s2 and s1), inflation expectations could de-anchor, and carry-trade unwinds could accelerate — a cross-cutting amplifier for multiple existing tracker stories.

indicators.label: BoJ political interference index
indicators.rubric: 0 = BoJ fully autonomous, no government language on rate path; 1 = government language nudges BoJ guidance or seeks GPIF rotation without formal mandate change; 2 = formal mandate change, government veto over rates, or snap election driven by monetary policy

indicators.label: US Fed independence stress
indicators.rubric: 0 = Fed chair credibly independent, forward guidance intact; 1 = forward guidance removed or chair under documented White House pressure; 2 = formal attempt to remove chair, Congressional override of Fed mandate, or rate decision reversed under political pressure

indicators.label: G7 central bank policy divergence (policy rate range, bps)
indicators.rubric: Range of G7 policy rates in bps; widening beyond 400bps signals disorderly divergence that strains FX and carry structures

new-risk-dm-central-bank-independence-un.rationale: No existing story captures the simultaneous political-interference dimension across both the US Fed and BoJ in the same reporting week. This is distinct from s2 (which covers Japan fiscal/JGB/carry mechanics) and s1 (Treasury basis trade) — it is about the credibility anchor itself. If both the world's two largest sovereign bond market central banks are perceived as politically captured in a high-debt/high-inflation environment, the tail risk is a simultaneous repricing of term premia in USTs and JGBs, which would cascade through s1, s2, s4, and s14. The IMF WEO explicitly warns that 'eroded policy buffers can amplify' downside risks. Proposed level 6 reflects that political language has been partly walked back but the structural risk is now observable and documented.
